Two: the Microsoft tie-in the title actually points to. CrowdStrike’s expanded strategic alliance with Microsoft enables Falcon purchases via Azure Marketplace, and AWS named it the first cybersecurity partner via SaaS Quick Launch in AWS Marketplace. When analysts cheer Microsoft’s chart, they are cheering a distribution channel CrowdStrike plugs directly into. Three: Falcon literally runs on the NVIDIA stack. Charlotte AI AgentWorks was built with NVIDIA technologies, and Falcon Cloud Security is integrated with NVIDIA NIM microservices and NeMo Safety. Same tailwind, different layer, different multiple. The fundamentals back the redirect. Q4 free cash flow came in at $376.36 million, a 29% margin. Falcon Flex ARR reached $1.69 billion, up over 120% YoY. Platform consolidation is visible across the customer base: 50% of customers run six or more modules, 34% run seven, 24% run eight. Management guided FY27 revenue to $5.87 billion to $5.93 billion, with a long-term aspiration of $20 billion in ending ARR by FY36. Keybanc moved to Overweight on April 21 with a $525 price target, and the analyst consensus target sits at $491.72 against a current price of $454.99. Reddit activity on CrowdStrike over the past month registered as “very_low” to “low” with only one to two qualified mentions per observation period. That silence is the tell. A retirement portfolio gets paid for owning the quiet, profitable company structurally tied to the loud trend everyone else is overpaying to access through one mega-cap ticker. Title: Blue Owl made 10x its money on SpaceX and has already sold half its position
URL: https://thenextweb.com/news/blue-owl-spacex-10x-return-ipo-1-75-trillion/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T17:28:29Z
Full Content:
The alternative asset manager’s co-CEO disclosed the returns on a Q1 earnings call, framing the SpaceX gain as a hedge against potential software credit losses from AI disruption, a revealing window into how private credit firms are navigating the AI era Blue Owl Capital has sold approximately half its SpaceX investment at a $1.25 trillion valuation, generating roughly 10 times its original investment on the realised portion while continuing to hold the remainder, co-CEO Marc Lipschultz disclosed on a Q1 2026 analyst call on Thursday. “Specifically at SpaceX, we made about 10 times our money on that investment,” Lipschultz said, according to Reuters. “We’ve sold about half of it at a $1.25 trillion valuation, still holding about half of it.” Blue Owl was one of SpaceX’s earliest institutional lenders and subsequently made an equity investment, purchasing shares in two classes in 2021 according to a 2025 securities filing. The 💜 of EU tech The latest rumblings from the EU tech scene, a story from our wise ol' founder Boris, and some questionable AI art. It's free, every week, in your inbox. Sign up now! The $1.25 trillion valuation at which it sold the first half corresponds to the post-merger figure established when SpaceX acquired Elon Musk’s AI company xAI in an all-stock transaction in February 2026, folding Grok, X (formerly Twitter), and xAI’s GPU infrastructure into the combined entity. If Blue Owl holds its remaining position to the SpaceX IPO, which is targeting a June 2026 listing at a possible valuation of $1.75 trillion and a $75 billion raise that would be the largest public offering in history, those unrealised gains would increase further still. The most revealing aspect of Lipschultz’s disclosure was not the 10x figure itself but the context in which he chose to cite it. He framed the SpaceX gain explicitly as a potential offset against losses in Blue Owl’s software loan portfolio, the concern being that the latest generation of AI models could displace some of the software companies to which Blue Owl has lent, causing defaults. This framing reflects a tension running through the private credit industry in 2026. Firms like Blue Owl, Ares, Apollo, and Blackstone have built enormous direct lending portfolios to technology and software companies, many of which operate in sectors now being disrupted by the very AI products that are simultaneously inflating the valuations of companies like SpaceX. The hedge Lipschultz described, SpaceX equity gains offsetting potential software credit losses, is not just a reassurance for analysts. It is a structural acknowledgement that private credit firms are now exposed to AI disruption risk from multiple directions simultaneously, and that equity upside in the AI infrastructure layer is one way to manage it. Blue Owl’s shares rose sharply on the earnings call, as investors reacted positively to the SpaceX disclosure and to the firm’s Q1 results: fee related earnings of $0.25 per share (up 14% year on year), distributable earnings of $0.19 per share (up 11%), and $11 billion in total capital raised in the quarter, with 67% from institutional investors and $3 billion from private wealth channels. Blue Owl’s disclosure arrives at a moment of maximum intensity in the SpaceX IPO process. As we covered this, SpaceX filed confidentially with the SEC on April 1, 2026, targeting a $75 billion raise at a valuation of $1.75 trillion — more than 2.5 times the Saudi Aramco IPO record of $29.4 billion set in 2019. The roadshow is expected to begin around June 8, with a major investor event on June 11. Lead underwriters include Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Bank of America, and Citigroup. If the IPO prices at $1.75 trillion, it would debut as one of the most valuable publicly listed companies on Earth. The $1.25 trillion figure at which Blue Owl sold half its position is itself significant: it is the valuation established by the SpaceX-xAI merger, not a market transaction. Blue Owl was therefore realising gains at a valuation set by an all-stock merger rather than through a fully liquid market-clearing price. The gap between that $1.25 trillion and the IPO target of $1.75 trillion represents the remaining paper upside on the half-position Blue Owl continues to hold, approximately $440 billion in incremental valuation if the IPO prices at the high end. For institutional investors in private credit funds, Blue Owl’s SpaceX story illustrates the evolution of the asset class over the past decade. What began as senior secured loans to established businesses has increasingly incorporated equity co-investments, preferred shares, and structured products that give funds exposure to the upside of private companies growing towards IPO. The 10x return on SpaceX equity, disclosed on the same day as a Meta bond offering and an Anthropic fundraising at near-$900 billion, underlines the degree to which 2026’s financial markets have become organised around a single underlying bet: that the current generation of AI infrastructure companies will be worth multiples of today’s already extraordinary valuations. I am the Editor in Chief for TNW, covering technology not as a parade of launches and valuations, but as a system of influence, persuasion, (show all) I am the Editor in Chief for TNW, covering technology not as a parade of launches and valuations, but as a system of influence, persuasion, and change. I write about startups, venture capital, digital policy, and Europe ecosystem, with an eye on the larger story beneath them: who gets to build the future, who profits from it, and how Europe is learning to speak in a louder voice of its own. Before moving into senior editorial leadership, I've built my career for over +10 years across journalism, storytelling, content strategy, SEO, and digital publishing, with experience in SaaS, hospitality, art, and culture. Title: LiveOne (Nasdaq: LVO) and Subsidiary PodcastOne (Nasdaq: PODC) Launch PodcastOneAI, Unlocking 200,000+ Hours Across $20B+ AI Training and $40B+ Data Monetization Markets
URL: https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/30/3285310/0/en/LiveOne-Nasdaq-LVO-and-Subsidiary-PodcastOne-Nasdaq-PODC-Launch-PodcastOneAI-Unlocking-200-000-Hours-Across-20B-AI-Training-and-40B-Data-Monetization-Markets.html
Time Published: 2026-04-30T17:19:00Z
Full Content:
April 30, 2026 13:19 ET | Source: PodcastOne PodcastOne LOS ANGELES, April 30, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- LiveOne (Nasdaq: LVO) and its subsidiary PodcastOne (Nasdaq: PODC), a leading podcast network and media platform, have launched PodcastOneAI, a proprietary AI platform designed to convert its extensive audio and video catalog into scalable, monetizable assets across the rapidly expanding artificial intelligence ecosystem. PodcastOneAI leverages more than 100,000 hours of premium, brand-safe content to meet increasing demand from hyperscalers, enterprise platforms, media companies and government organizations seeking high-quality training data and licensed content solutions. The platform is structured to generate multiple high-margin revenue streams, including upfront licensing agreements, recurring royalties, advertising integrations and strategic sponsorships, while expanding distribution across AI and large language model ecosystems. “PodcastOneAI represents a transformative step in unlocking the full value of our content library,” said Robert Ellin, Executive Chairman of PodcastOne. “AI models face content shortages as Disney, Paramount, Netflix and Warner restrict access driving urgent demand from Google, Meta and Amazon for licensed content bundles. We are positioning PodcastOne at the center of the AI content economy by making our premium catalog AI-ready, enabling scalable monetization while significantly expanding our global reach.” About LiveOneHeadquartered in Los Angeles, CA, LiveOne (Nasdaq: LVO) is an award-winning, creator-first, music, entertainment, and technology platform focused on delivering premium experiences and content worldwide and live and virtual events. LiveOne's subsidiaries include Slacker, PodcastOne (Nasdaq: PODC), PPVOne, Custom Personalization Solutions, LiveXLive and DayOne Music Publishing. LiveOne is available on iOS, Android, Roku, Apple TV, Spotify, Samsung, Amazon Fire, Android TV, and through STIRR's OTT applications. For more information, visit liveone.com and follow us on Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube and Twitter at @liveone. For more investor information, please visit ir.liveone.com. About PodcastOnePodcastOne (Nasdaq: PODC) is a leading podcast platform that provides creators and advertisers with a comprehensive 360-degree solution in sales, marketing, public relations, production, and distribution. PodcastOne has surpassed 3.9 billion total downloads with a community of 200 top podcasters, including Adam Carolla, Kaitlyn Bristowe, Jordan Harbinger, LadyGang, A&E's Cold Case Files, and Varnamtown. PodcastOne has built a distribution network reaching over 1 billion monthly impressions across all channels, including YouTube, Spotify, Apple Podcasts, and iHeartRadio. PodcastOne is also the parent company of PodcastOne Pro which offers fully customizable production packages for brands, professionals, or hobbyists. For more information, visit www.podcastone.com and follow us on Facebook, Instagram, YouTube, and X at @podcastone. These forward-looking statements speak only as of the date hereof, and LiveOne disclaims any obligation to update these statements, except as may be required by law. LiveOne intends that all forward-looking statements be subject to the safe-harbor provisions of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. * About Non-GAAP Financial MeasuresTo supplement our consolidated financial statements, which are prepared and presented in accordance with the accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America (“GAAP”), we present Contribution Margin (Loss) and Adjusted Earnings Before Interest Tax Depreciation and Amortization (“Adjusted EBITDA”), which are non-GAAP financial measures, as measures of our performance. The presentation of these non-GAAP financial measures is not intended to be considered in isolation from, or as a substitute for, or superior to, operating loss and or net income (loss) or any other performance measures derived in accordance with GAAP or as an alternative to net cash provided by operating activities or any other measures of our cash flows or liquidity. We use Contribution Margin (Loss) and Adjusted EBITDA to evaluate the performance of our operating segments. We believe that information about these non-GAAP financial measures assists investors by allowing them to evaluate changes in the operating results of our business separate from non-operational factors that affect operating income (loss) and net income (loss), thus providing insights into both operations and the other factors that affect reported results. Adjusted EBITDA is not calculated or presented in accordance with GAAP. A limitation of the use of Adjusted EBITDA as a performance measure is that it does not reflect the periodic costs of certain amortizing assets used in generating revenue in our business. Accordingly, Adjusted EBITDA should be considered in addition to, and not as a substitute for operating income (loss), net income (loss), and other measures of financial performance reported in accordance with GAAP. Furthermore, this measure may vary among other companies; thus, Adjusted EBITDA as presented herein may not be comparable to similarly titled measures of other companies. Contribution Margin (Loss) is defined as Revenue less Cost of Sales before (a) Cost of Sales share-based compensation expense, (b) depreciation, and (c) amortization of developed technology. Adjusted EBITDA is defined as earnings before interest, other (income) expense, income tax expense, depreciation and amortization and before (a) non-cash GAAP purchase accounting adjustments for certain deferred revenue and costs, (b) legal, accounting and other professional fees directly attributable to acquisition activity, (c) employee severance payments and third party professional fees directly attributable to acquisition or corporate realignment activities, (d) certain non-recurring expenses associated with legal settlements or reserves for legal settlements in the period that pertain to historical matters that existed at acquired companies prior to their purchase date and a one-time minimum guarantee to effectively terminate a live events distribution agreement post COVID-19, and (e) certain stock-based compensation expense. Management does not consider these costs to be indicative of our core operating results. With respect to projected quarter, nine-month and full Fiscal 2026 Adjusted EBITDA, a quantitative reconciliation is not available without unreasonable efforts due to the high variability, complexity and low visibility with respect to purchase accounting adjustments, acquisition-related charges and legal settlement reserves excluded from Adjusted EBITDA. We expect that the variability of these items to have a potentially unpredictable, and potentially significant, impact on our future GAAP financial results. Title: JPMorgan Downgrades Meta to Neutral: Are AI CapEx Concerns Finally Catching Up?
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/04/30/jpmorgan-downgrades-meta-to-neutral-are-ai-capex-concerns-finally-catching-up/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T16:45:24Z
Full Content:
Investing JPMorgan downgraded Meta Platforms (META) to Neutral from Overweight, citing full-stack AI competition and unclear returns on escalating CapEx despite strong 33% YoY Q1 revenue growth. Meta raised its full-year CapEx guidance to $125B-$145B from $115B-$135B amid higher component and data center costs, forcing investors to weigh aggressive AI spending against uncertain returns. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Meta wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Derick Hudson / iStock Editorial via Getty Images Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META | META Price Prediction) stock fell after JPMorgan downgraded it to Neutral from Overweight, cutting the price target to $725 from $825. The call came the morning after a blowout Q1 2026 earnings report, framing a sharp tension for investors: aggressive artificial intelligence capital expenditure (CapEx) versus visible returns. The rating cut diverges from peers. Cantor Fitzgerald, TD Cowen, and Barclays adjusted their price targets while keeping bullish ratings on Meta Platforms stock, signaling a valuation reset rather than a broken thesis. JPMorgan acknowledges being encouraged by Meta Platforms’ 33% YoY revenue growth in Q1 2026, supported by AI-driven advertising strength. However, the firm flags rising infrastructure spending and limited visibility into the AI product pipeline as reasons for the downgrade. The deeper concern is competitive. Full-stack AI rivals like Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) are intensifying, making the path to returns on heavy AI CapEx beyond advertising tougher to underwrite for Meta Platforms. Peers see the same data differently. Cantor Fitzgerald cut its META stock price target to $750 from $850 with Overweight; TD Cowen lowers to $800 from $820 with Buy; and Barclays raises to $830 from $800, citing Meta Platforms growing twice as fast as the digital ad industry. Meta Platforms carries a market capitalization of $1.33 trillion with a trailing P/E ratio of 24x. Q1 2026 EPS came in at $10.44 versus estimates of $6.66, inflated by an $8.03 billion tax benefit. Meanwhile, Meta Platforms’ revenue reached $56.31 billion, ad impressions rose 19% YoY, and Family daily active people hit 3.56 billion. Reality Labs posted a $4.03 billion operating loss. Meta Platforms’ full-year CapEx guidance rose to $125 billion to $145 billion, from $115 billion to $135 billion, citing higher component pricing and data center costs. Q1 CapEx alone hit $19 billion, up 47% YoY. A comparison to Meta Platforms’ peers is worth making. Google Cloud revenue jumped to $20.03 billion with backlog near $460 billion, while Amazon’s AWS grew 28% with $37.59 billion in revenue, its fastest pace in 15 quarters. Polymarket participants assigned a 100% probability to META stock closing down on April 30, validating the sentiment shift. For an analysis of infrastructure beneficiaries, see our underrated 2026 chip-and-EV winner coverage. For prudent investors, this downgrade reads as a recalibration of expectations around AI returns. Meta Platforms still posts industry-leading margins and outgrows the digital ad industry, yet the AI return-on-investment clock is now ticking publicly. Watch for whether the Meta Superintelligence Labs roadmap delivers monetizable products and whether 2026 operating income holds above 2025 levels as guided. Position sizing should reflect both regulatory tail risks and the elevated CapEx run rate. The new $725 price target still implies room above current levels, suggesting JPMorgan’s call concerns the pace of AI returns. Prudent investors may treat this as a reason to trim, rather than abandon, exposure to Meta Platforms stock. Title: The Outlaws Who Keep the Power Grid Running
URL: https://www.esquire.com/news-politics/a71078454/power-grid-outlaws/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T16:33:14Z
Full Content:
Over the last decade, an army of hard-living adrenaline junkies made a fortune and found a kind of freedom maintaining the nation’s energy infrastructure. It was good while it lasted. Last August, in Seminole, Florida, a suburb of St. Petersburg, a crew of power linemen were replacing a utility pole at a small electrical substation next to a used-car dealership. The temperature was pushing 90 degrees, but the sky was clear; an ideal Florida beach day. Just before 2:00 p.m., they strapped the replacement metal pole to the boom on their truck and hoisted it vertically into the air. Setting a new pole is an act of both muscularity and balletic precision, but for experienced linemen, it’s a typical task. The new pole must be held steady at the bottom while an operator in the truck uses a claw on the end of the boom to maneuver it into a waiting hole. When done right, linemen can replace multiple utility poles in a day, but things in Seminole didn’t go as planned: When the boom operator attempted to grab the pole with the claw, he bumped against it instead, causing the top of the pole to accidentally make contact with the high-voltage transmission lines running overhead. The explosion that followed was so violent it lit the earth itself on fire. Sixty-nine thousand volts, enough to feed the substation, powering entire neighborhoods, diverted from the wire into the new pole and rocketed toward the ground. In the current’s way stood Justin Bryan, a thirty-year-old father and music director at his local church, who was balancing the pole from below. The current ripped through Bryan’s body, killing him. It continued flowing into the ground around the truck, causing the grassy field to burst into flames, smoke billowing skyward. Two other linemen, Jefferey Busch and Matthew Waldrop, fathers in their late twenties and early thirties, were also in contact with the pole and were left crawling on the charred earth, horrifically burned but alive. Bystanders could do little to help; so long as the wires remained energized, electricity could still arc and flow anywhere in the vicinity. I first saw an eyewitness video of the accident’s horrific aftermath this past December, not long after I had arrived at the headquarters of Pack Power Services, a utility-services company nestled among the horse farms of Ohio Amish country. Work had been slow; a thin dusting of snow coated the few dozen bucket trucks and pickups in the lot outside the company’s warehouse. When I started reporting on the lives of linemen late last year, I quickly discovered that although linemen are on the front lines of maintaining the power grid—our connection to medical care, drinking water, cell phones, the Internet, emergency services, lights, heat, you name it—no one knows much about them. Most people assumed I was writing about football. But the people who did know what a lineman was kept telling me to talk to the same person: Pack Power’s owner, Jerry Pack. Colleagues told me that he was the quintessential lineman—an Army vet and a biker; rough around the edges, sure, but loyal to a fault. But he was also more than an archetype. Jerry had helped revolutionize the trade and ridden its ups and downs for years. When I got in touch, Jerry, who looks a bit like the actor Brendan Gleeson if Brendan Gleeson had a graying ZZ Top beard and tattoos down his forearms, invited me to come visit on a Thursday—“That’s when we drink,” he said—and greeted me at the warehouse door with a lit Marlboro Red between his fingers. He had also invited a few other linemen with stories to tell, including Nick Mansfield, from East Texas, who quickly pulled me aside and asked if I wanted to see the “real shit” about being a lineman. He pulled his cell phone from his pocket and hit play on a copy of the Seminole video. I lasted only a few seconds before I had to look away. Nick, who met Jerry years ago on the job and remains one of his closest friends, is a lineman’s lineman. He’s burly and bearded and wears a chain around his neck that, at first glance, appears to be a traditional Christ on the cross but isn’t Jesus at all. Rather, it’s a lineman climbing a utility pole. While the incident in Seminole was barely more than a blip in the local news, he told me linemen across the country had seen the video. There’s an old expression, he said: “Telegraph, telephone, tell a lineman.” Word travels fast in the industry, and the industry, lately, had heard too many stories like this. A lineman dies on the job on average once every two weeks, ranking it among the deadliest professions in America. While Jerry and Nick showed me around the warehouse, we discussed the incident in Seminole. The Pinellas County Sheriff’s Office investigated and determined it was an accident, while OSHA recently levied $49,650 in penalties, which is being contested by the contractor hired by the utility company, Duke Energy. But as the video made its way from the jobsite to the union hall to TikTok, linemen in the comments section became fixated on the fact that the transmission line was energized during the work. Jerry and Nick told me that they would have demanded the utility de-energize the lines before getting started, a precaution that would have stopped all the meters in all the homes and businesses downstream of the substation from spinning—and from generating revenue. (When contacted about the details of the incident, Duke Energy replied with this statement: “The safety of our employees, contractors and customers remains Duke Energy’s highest priority, and we continue to cooperate fully with the ongoing investigation process.”) Jerry understood, all too well, why so many linemen who saw the video scrutinized the safety practices that were in place. The men in Seminole had been doing what’s called “blue sky” work, regular repair and maintenance of the power grid. But the industry’s relentless push toward high speed and low cost were most evident in the niche kind of linework that Jerry specialized in: storm chasing. In this run-and-gun model of linework, independent crews show up whenever and wherever they are needed to get the power back on after hurricanes, blizzards, and wildfires. Multiple linemen described the storm-chasing world to me as the “Wild West,” a place where the allure of big money attracted roguish personalities. For years, the living was good, great even, but to hear Jerry and Nick tell it, the frontier was closing. America’s electrical grid was becoming more critical to daily life than ever, and the opportunity to rake in profits had attracted middleman contractors and private-equity investment. For the storm chasers, that meant pressure to perform ratcheting ever higher, and their livelihoods coming under threat as the incentives to do whatever it took to secure contracts—aboveboard or otherwise—grew stronger. For the rest of us? It meant the future of the grid itself was at stake. As the sun went down, the three of us drove the few minutes to Jerry’s ranch house, where we settled in around the kitchen table. Jerry’s girlfriend, Rita, who had already set out foil trays filled with pigs in a blanket and chicken wings on the stovetop, offered us pint glasses of vodka and cranberry juice. I asked Jerry when things started to change. “About ten years ago,” he said as he filled a paper plate with wings. “Irma was the fire under my ass.” In 2017, Jerry was pulling into his driveway after work one day when he discovered his then-wife, Sally, waiting in the doorway in tears. “It’s cancer,” she told him. Her doctor had just called with the test results they’d been waiting for. Over the previous eighteen years, Jerry had worked his way up from an apprenticeship to doing blue-sky work at a substation as a contractor for a local utility. It was a good job with a regular schedule. Sally’s diagnosis changed everything. He’d need to be available to drive her to treatment and support her when she got home. He decided on the spot to drag up—quit without notice, in lineman-speak—and take to the road. “I’ll just storm-chase,” he told her. At the time, most power-restoration efforts were handled by mutual-aid agreements between utilities. If a hurricane hit Florida, a utility company in North Carolina might send some crews to help, understanding that Florida would return the favor if needed. A few large private energy companies had their own crews. Jerry started putting in calls. If linework is the Wild West, storm chasers are the outlaws of the bunch. They’re the guys lauded as heroes on local-news broadcasts for restoring power after natural disasters, but among their ranks you can find all kinds, from drug-addled ex-felons to former lawyers and everything in between. Some storm chasers, affectionately known as tramps, essentially live on the road full-time, traveling the country in RVs or tricked-out pickup trucks, going wherever they’re needed. They keep two drag bags packed at all times: one for their gear—harnesses, hard hats, and boot spikes for climbing utility poles—and the other stuffed with a week’s worth of clothes. Walker Pettis, a tramp from Washington, told me he’s moved thirty times in the past five years. No sooner had Jerry decided to join their ranks than the strongest hurricane in recent memory began intensifying in the southern Atlantic. The meteorologists called it Irma. “THIS IS AS REAL AS IT GETS,” wrote the National Weather Service in a tweet the night before Irma struck. Residents were urged to flee. In the largest exodus in American history, some seven million Floridians got in their cars and drove north, leaving their homes, businesses, and belongings behind. Jerry, on the other hand, jumped in a truck and headed south toward Miami, amid a caravan of vehicles carrying linemen from across the country. He had gotten a call from an acquaintance asking if he could lead a crew. At his day job before storms, Jerry usually ran with four other guys; in Florida, he’d be in charge of 120. “I was scared to fucking death, dude,” he said. In Miami, Jerry and his crew holed up in a hotel and watched through the windows as Irma transformed the world around them: waves of water breaching the Miami River seawall, palm trees blown horizontal in the gale, alligators wandering suburban sidewalks. Nearly two thirds of the state was left without power. Some sixty thousand linemen had descended on the state, and the utility companies scrambled to get organized. The linemen were happy to wait; they were getting paid whether they left the hotel or not. Chaos is cash, as linemen like to say, and Irma had brought the chaos. President Trump described the response as “the largest assemblage of human beings ever in one area for power.” Linemen faced floodwaters, live wires dangling amid the trees, and bird dogs from the utility companies—the office workers tasked with directing crews to their next work site—pressuring them to work on high-voltage lines in gusts of wind and rain. While they moved from one downed pole or blown-out transformer to the next, Jerry and his crew were approached by elderly couples desperate for help and by countless residents demanding to have their TVs turned back on. In a little over a week, the linemen had fully restored power in a state of twenty million people. Public officials hailed them as heroes. Jerry didn’t feel like a hero until he deposited his paycheck. His stress finally abated. “Don’t sweat it,” he told Sally. “We’re all set.” Irma changed everything for Jerry, and it changed everything for linemen. The traditional mutual-aid model was effective, but it could be slow. Pack Power, which Jerry started after returning from Irma, was one of the country’s first linemen-owned businesses to offer something different: rapid response. Jerry’s guys could be sent anywhere they were needed at a moment’s notice. Within a few years, a post-Irma surge of these kinds of outfits had revolutionized the industry. “Years ago, there were no companies that just did storm work,” said a stockbroker-turned-lineman who left what he described as the “scumbag business” of Wall Street and now runs a storm-recovery business on the East Coast. “Today, I’d only be guessing, but I would say there’s probably close to between six hundred to a thousand companies that do just that.” After we’d been talking for an hour, Randy Wolfe slipped into the kitchen. A slight, quiet guy in his early forties who lived nearby, he sat down on a kitchen stool and pulled a Modelo from some hidden recess of his lime-yellow reflective work jacket. Randy epitomized the stormchasing lineman of the post-Irma era, who were in control of their schedules and making fortunes unthinkable to their blue-sky brethren. His taste in beer may be lowbrow, but he owns a collection of high-end sports cars, including a C5 Corvette and a Lotus Elise. More than once, the quiet roads around Jerry’s house had been lit up by wailing sirens and flashing lights as Randy outran the cops in one of his supercars. Randy described the gaps between jobs like this: “I pick a car and I go to the lake all day. If the phone rings, fine. If it don’t ring, fantastic.” For this new generation of linemen, storm-chasing became as much a lifestyle as a livelihood, built on the twin pillars of money and freedom. “We’re like modern-day pirates,” said Nick. At first, all Jerry had wanted was the freedom to bring Sally to her oncology appointments in Chicago without worrying about sick days or PTO. But once the money started rolling in, he bought a few Porsches and some rental properties and expanded his fleet of bucket trucks. The indoor hot tub he installed at his house was so big he had to build a new room to go around it. In order to maintain this newfound abundance, storm chasers relied on a steady stream of what’s known in the business as Good Storms. What exactly makes a Good Storm is up for debate, though time is the obvious answer. A Good Storm lasts a couple weeks at least. The longest can go on for a month or more. For linemen working sixteen-hour days, seven days a week, a Good Storm can yield a paycheck on par with the average American annual salary. Jerry told me that during Irma—one of the best storms in recent memory—the most experienced guys on his crew, which was unionized, were being paid around $150 an hour, double or even triple the going rate back home. That’s $16,800 a week, not including food, travel, and a place to sleep. Though most linemen will proudly recount stories of residents thanking them for getting their power back on after a disaster, it’s clear they’re not in it for the goodwill alone. “This isn’t a humanitarian effort,” as one lineman told me. Nick’s been doing it for so long he now refers to the Weather Channel as “the Money Channel.” Linemen have been known to quit salaried positions without notice just to join a single storm roster during hurricane season. This wayward streak has long been associated with linework. Nick’s “line dad”—his mentor—told him that the roots of the linemen’s renegade spirit can be found in the Vietnam vets who returned from the war and joined the ranks because they felt out of place in society. But the storm-chasing lifestyle brought it to new heights. After driving long hours in caravans of bucket trucks, linemen ride out storms everywhere from empty hotel rooms to hammocks in the backs of their trucks to military-style tent-and-cot setups known as “man camps.” These overnights offer the chance for a lineman’s worst instincts to flourish—hard drinking, affairs with local women dubbed “bucket bunnies,” even crime. Linemen on storm have been accused of everything from felony assault to trafficking drug shipments in their trucks. “There is a culture in linework that is seedy, romanticized, drug ridden,” the former stockbroker said. “There’s a lot of guys that are looking for the next high.” Nick once had a crewmate who found himself face-to-face with an angry pimp after unceremoniously kicking a sex worker out of his hotel room in the middle of the night. A fight ensued and the pimp ended up both beaten up and under arrest. “The lineman was at work at seven in the fucking morning,” Nick recalled. And yet, despite all the potential for misadventure, the shared unpleasantness of sleepless nights and long waits for food at the mess tent had a way of turning colleagues into comrades. “We meet guys one time,” said Nick, “work a storm for a week, and you become lifelong friends.” Nick’s been sober for half a year now, and all night Jerry subtly topped him off with water, not wanting him to stare at an empty glass while we downed our cocktails. Linework was simply too dangerous to do anything but have each other’s back, Jerry told me. “Your ego can’t get in that.” Randy lit up describing how a crowd of linemen had gathered in awe and respect when he and his father ran into each other on storm and set a utility pole together. “It was one of the coolest things I’ve ever been a part of,” he said, pulling another Modelo from his vest. He’d keep drinking them all night. They were a merry band in those days. They saw themselves as something like outlaws, because they’d found a way to make money hand over fist living an itinerant, rambunctious lifestyle that suited them. They were getting away with something. But the hard-partying, carefree life was about to get a lot harder. “Those days are about gone,” Jerry said, wistfully. Someone with an even better way to turn chaos into cash was riding into town. Jaradda Sparks sensed an opportunity. The forward-thinking lineman from Alabama saw more and more storm chasers following Jerry in opening their own companies. He also saw utilities struggling to manage them. He went straight to the utility companies and promised to save them time and money by aggregating the services of hundreds of smaller storm-chasing companies under the umbrella of his own, Sparks Energy. If a storm hit, a power company only had to make one call, to Sparks, to secure the services of thousands of linemen spread out across the country. No more shopping or logistics; Sparks was a one-stop shop. He charged the utilities a hefty premium, but they were clearly willing to pay: By 2019, he had made enough money to open his own livestock ranch, specializing in breeding “top-caliber bucking bulls” for the rodeo circuit. This new aggregator model initially had a beneficial trickle-down effect. Sparks declined to speak with me for this article, but I heard from many linemen that while his approach was designed to cater to utilities, it also funneled jobs to storm chasers in his network, who could work dozens of storms a year without the legwork of going out and bidding for new business. Jerry grossed $15 million one of the years he worked under Sparks as a subcontractor. But the model was so successful that aggregators soon multiplied, just as the storm-chasing outfits once had, creating competition that made life harder for guys like Jerry. In order to lock in higher premiums from utility companies, aggregators tried to corner the market, securing ironclad contracts that ensured utilities would work only with them. Independent companies found it increasingly challenging to land contracts of their own. In intervening years, the industry took on a new shape. There was still a ton of money to be made, but now there was a lot more competition, a new layer of middlemen, and more complicated contracts. This environment cut into the bottom lines of companies like Pack Power. It also opened the door to bad actors. None of the dozen active linemen I spoke to for this story were willing to go on the record about corruption in the industry, but they’ve worked all over the country and nearly all had stories. One storm chaser told me a company he’d worked for hauled broken-down bucket trucks on wreckers to storms because they were being paid per truck, even if the trucks sat in a parking lot unused. Another lineman told me he had been sent out on a storm crew without the proper tools or dielectric testing to make sure the trucks were insulated in case of an accident. When the crews came back an hour later, unable to work, he still got paid. Less visible backroom deals were also in play. One storm-company owner told me explicitly that he paid $15,000 for an executive at a power company to go on an overseas trip in exchange for better work contracts, only for the executive to ghost him once he had returned. The cutthroat competition in the industry incentivized these tactics, and the problems were perpetuated when word, inevitably, got out. Telegraph, telephone, tell a lineman. These shifting practices may also have put lives at risk. At least one lineman I spoke to who asked not to be named told me that some aggregators were known for hiring “guys who weren’t qualified to do the work and going out with trucks that didn’t have any tooling on them.” Jerry’s business suddenly began to suffer. After a couple of years in the black, he found that he was getting fewer calls from aggregators. He told me that inexperienced, nonunion linemen were willing to work for less money per hour than his guys, brothers in the IBEW. Then it stopped being standard for aggregators to pay linemen for mobilization and travel days, forcing those costs to come out of Jerry’s bottom line instead. All this, and many questioned whether the aggregator model was actually saving anyone any money. Average electricity prices are up more than 30 percent across the country in the last five years alone. Are the changes in linework the reason why? It’s hard to say for certain—but it certainly hasn’t helped. Looking back, the storm chasers should have known the end of their era was near when the bucket bunnies broke the Internet. In 2022, a Florida TikToker revealed that in the weeks following Hurricane Ian, her Tinder account had become overrun with linemen heading to the Sunshine State for storm work. “Everyone saying pray for Florida but this is our tinder right now,” she wrote before the reel revealed that, extensive storm damage aside, things in the state maybe weren’t so bad: There was a stream of handsome, well-paid linemen in town looking for dates. Other users followed suit, posting videos of linemen mobilizing for work outside hotels and in parking lots. Then the “linewives,” the married partners of linemen, arrived, posting their own TikToks railing against the bucket bunnies who were out to steal their men. The melodrama was hugely popular. The bucket-bunny trend lasted for months and was viewed nearly three billion times, landing it just behind the Ice Bucket Challenge as one of the most popular memes of the past few years. It spawned merchandise; online commenters begged for a reality-TV show. The linemen, however, were unenthused. As word got out about how much money they were making, sleeping with women while on storm became a lot less carefree. Nick, in his inimitable way, took the opportunity to explain the other three T’s of linework: “You buy your girl new teeth, new tits, and a brand-new Tahoe, before she walks out on you,” he said. Some apprentices, he told me, stopped letting women know they were linemen. Social media had transformed bucket bunnies, at one time the consummate example of the linemen’s freewheeling lifestyle, into a new kind of peril, one that could lead to divorce or something even worse: child support. Of course, if enough money was flying around that bucket bunnies could sniff it out, it was only a matter of time before the real bloodhounds showed up: Wall Street. In the past few years alone, high-powered private-equity firms like BlackRock and Blackstone have found ways to get their cut. Attracted by the potential for a huge, data-center-driven increase in energy, they’ve spent billions buying up major utility providers in places like Minnesota, Indiana, New Mexico, and abroad. According to one count, some 150 million customers in the U. S. now rely on utilities funded at least in part by private-equity money. Under the PE model, buying into the companies that service those utilities is simply another layer of vertical integration to leverage. Just last year, Apollo Global Management spent roughly $2 billion to acquire a stake in PowerGrid Services, a prominent grid-repair and maintenance company founded by a former lineman in 2016. Investments like these are touted as necessary to improve the grid’s infrastructure. Last July, BlackRock’s CEO, Larry Fink, said as much in an interview on CNBC. “We believe that there’s a need for trillions of dollars investing in infrastructure related to our power grids, AI, the whole digitization of the economy,” said Fink. But how big money’s incursion into the grid will affect both linemen and ratepayers is less clear. According to industry studies, the average length of power outages in 2025 was 40 percent longer than in the three years prior. Part of that is down to the increasing severity and frequency of the storms themselves. But an experienced workforce of linemen is essential to the recovery effort. And for them, private equity has been even worse than for customers. In the last two years alone, nonunion linemen have seen pay shrink, while call times to mobilize for storms have shortened from a few days to a few hours. “In the beginning of my storm-chasing career,” Jerry explained, “you had a reasonable time to get there. See the lady, get packed up, take her out to eat. Get on a plane the next day.” Now, he continued, “I have to have guys at the shop ready to leave at all times.” This sometimes means defying DOT rules limiting the number of hours bucket-truck drivers can spend on the road. Once they’ve arrived, the already fatigued linemen are still expected to work sixteen-hour days, seven days a week, until the job is done. So far, linemen have been spared their Perfect Storm moment; there’s no mass tragedy that defines the trade. Instead, the specter of death arrives in a slow, steady cadence. In April of last year, a lineman was killed in Tennessee when his truck overturned into floodwaters; a few weeks later, four linemen were killed in North Carolina when a city dump truck crashed into their work site; in May, a falling tree in Alabama killed a lineman where he stood; in July, a lineman was electrocuted a few miles from where the Seminole tragedy unfolded a month later; in October, a hit-and-run; November, another electrocution, this time in Louisville. A number of fatal incidents show how pressure to cut corners and use junior crews can make the job even more perilous. In recent years, OSHA has assessed penalties of more than $40,000 and claimed that the lack of proper safety precautions led to at least two separate fatal incidents. In one, an electric company “failed to meet their responsibility to ensure a safe and healthful workplace and ensure the proper supervision of new workers.” That incident, in Alabama, resulted in the death of two nineteen-year-old apprentices. In the other, in Miami, a contractor “failed to use required safeguards before starting work,” and a thirty-two-year-old lineman died. Earlier in the evening, I had asked Jerry about the worst accident he had ever seen. He sighed and slumped slightly in his chair; Nick shot me a glance. Don’t go there. But when I brought it up again later, I got a different response: indignation at how the blame for injuries and deaths always seemed to fall at the feet of the linemen themselves, as opposed to, say, contractors or utility companies pressuring crews to work despite safety concerns. “Doesn’t matter what happens, who dies,” Nick said. “The problem can never get back to the power company.” Despite the contested fine levied by OSHA in the Seminole incident, the families of all three victims have had to fend for themselves, starting GoFundMe campaigns to help cover the cost of their recovery. It’s something Anne-Marie Olivieri, a linewife, has seen all too often. She founded the LineLife Foundation to help raise money for linemen killed on the job and as part of her effort tracks every injury and death she can, compiling lists and sharing them among linemen and their families. According to her, the tallies are rarely complete. “A lot of these are swept under the rug or they don’t get reported,” she told me. “There’s been times where I’ve actually gotten pictures of the accident and the company has threatened me.” Wary of a lawsuit, she declined to discuss the particulars, but her experience informs a fear among linemen that in this new private-equity world, shareholder profit, not outage times or lineman safety, will be the new benchmark for success. Nick, indelibly, described how linemen feel. After an accident, he said, “they’ll post your job before they post your obituary.” Around 11:00 p.m., Jerry pushed back from the kitchen table, stood up, and announced it was time to go to the hot-tub room. In there, Nick lit up a cigar while Jerry showed me his collection of Donald Trump–styled rubber duckies and bottles of Pappy Van Winkle bourbon. Like the Vietnam vets who had found a home in linework before him, Jerry had spun a decent day job into his own American dream: freedom, money, and the open road. But it was fleeting. The salad days of $15 million in gross are long gone. This article appeared in the April/May 2026 issue of Esquiresubscribe So far, Jerry is uninterested in cashing in on the private-equity money-go-round, partly, it seems, due to pride. Money and freedom, both valuable things, aren’t quite the same. “There was a time when I had to roll my own fucking cigarettes,” he told me. “I built this, I made every fucking bit of this.” He’d rather hand off Pack Power Services to his kids than to a Wall Street firm. Chris, his eldest son, is already in a leadership role, handling much of the company’s day-to-day responsibilities, including storm work. PJ, another son, is the in-house mechanic. Jerry’s daughter stands to inherit his rental properties. And while he and Sally, recovered from cancer, split up a few years ago, she lives just a few minutes away in a house Jerry says he paid for. This future is dependent on whether or not the company survives. Like nearly all the other businesses started by former linemen whose owners I spoke to, the injection of big money into the industry has made it difficult for Pack Power to compete. In the good ol’ days, Jerry had millions in the bank and no debt. “Now I’ve got fifty grand in the bank and I’m two million in the hole,” he told me. Jerry still has the lineman’s hustle in his blood. He’s working on a deal to help the FAA modernize the infrastructure at a nearby airport. And there’s this other thing. “As soon as I saw Russia invading Ukraine, I knew we, as the United States, were going to be rebuilding their power grid,” he told me. “I’d love to be on that.” I didn’t know how seriously to take him, considering we were surrounded by empty beer cans and a haze of cigar and cigarette smoke. But as it turns out, Jerry had in fact spent much of the past year developing plans with a partner in the UK to join the international effort to rebuild Ukraine’s power grid, whenever that day comes. It was nearing midnight when I left Jerry’s, the image of a crew of American linemen stepping off a plane in postwar Kyiv, just as they’d rushed into California after a wildfire or Florida after Irma, running through my head. The Wild West had been tamed, so they’d have to go find a new frontier. Outside, it was dark; there weren’t many streetlights in the neighborhood. But as I drove past the Pack Power warehouse on the way to my hotel, it was a different sight that stirred something in me: a long row of bucket trucks parked in the lot out front, ready and waiting for the next job, still coated in snow but illuminated under the warehouse lights. Title: Meta Stock Loses $175 Billion After AI Expense Estimate Shakes Shareholders
URL: https://beincrypto.com/meta-stock-drops-despite-positive-earnings/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T16:11:07Z
Full Content:
Meta Platforms (META) shares dropped roughly 10% on Thursday, erasing about $175 billion in market value. A higher 2026 capital expenditure forecast of $125 billion to $145 billion triggered the selloff. The decline marked the stock’s largest single-day percentage drop in roughly six months. It came despite Q1 2026 earnings that exceeded Wall Street estimates on both revenue and profit. As of this writing, META stock was trading for $606.43, down by almost 10% in the last 24 hours, wiping out up top $175 billion from its market cap today alone. The new spending range sits roughly 7% above the previous January guidance of $115 billion to $135 billion. Chief Financial Officer Susan Li attributed the increase to higher memory-chip pricing. She also cited additional data center costs tied to artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. $META CFO Susan Li says headcount reductions that they announced last week will help offset increased spending in AI infrastructure. My take: Latest data point that AI is having an impact on employment. JPMorgan analyst Doug Anmuth downgraded Meta to Neutral and cut the bank’s price target to $725 from $825. The note flagged intensifying full-stack AI competition and a more challenging path to returns. Q1 capex alone reached $19.8 billion, in line with the broader Big Tech race in AI infrastructure. Meta reported revenue of $56.31 billion, up 33% year over year, the strongest quarterly growth since 2021. Net income reached $26.8 billion, or $10.44 per diluted share. An $8 billion one-time tax benefit tied to U.S. Treasury R&D guidance lifted that figure. *AMAZON 1Q NET SALES $181.52B, EST. $177.23B*AMAZON SEES 2Q NET SALES $194.0B TO $199.0B, EST. $189.15B*META 1Q REV. $56.31B, EST. $55.51B*META SEES 2Q REV. $58B TO $61B, EST. $59.56B*MICROSOFT 3Q REV. $82.89B, EST. $81.46B*MICROSOFT 3Q OPER INCOME $38.40B, EST. $36.9B… Ad revenue stayed strong as AI-powered content recommendations boosted engagement on Reels and video. Yet the reaction echoed earlier sell-offs after prior Meta capex hikes. The pattern repeatedly overshadows strong fundamentals with spending fears. CEO Mark Zuckerberg defended the strategy on the call. He framed the higher outlay as a vote of confidence in Meta’s AI roadmap. Former Twitter CEO Parag Agrawal's new company, Parallel Web Systems, has secured $100 million in funding. This investment values the startup at $2 billion. Parallel Web Systems is developing advanced infrastructure for AI agents to efficiently search the internet. This marks a significant comeback for Agrawal, who was ousted from Twitter. Title: Buy, Sell or Hold NFLX Stock After the Dip?
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/04/30/buy-sell-or-hold-nflx-stock-after-the-dip/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T15:55:43Z
Full Content:
Investing Netflix (NFLX) is a Hold at $92.37; entry point not generous enough despite intact bull case. Netflix’s advertising business scaling rapidly toward $3 billion with 60% of new signups and 70% YoY advertiser growth. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Netflix wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © hocus-focus / iStock Unreleased via Getty Images At $92.37, Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX | NFLX Price Prediction) is a Hold. The stock has been a top-tier compounder for a decade, but the post-earnings dip has not made it cheap enough to chase when better-priced opportunities exist. Netflix runs the world’s largest paid streaming service with 325 million plus subscribers. Q1 2026 revenue grew 16.2% YoY to $12.25 billion and beat consensus, while EPS came in at $1.23 and also beat estimates. A $2.8 billion termination fee from the abandoned Warner Bros. acquisition flattered net income and free cash flow. The stock has since drifted, leaving NFLX down 16.9% over the past year while the S&P 500 climbed 29.2%. Netflix is guiding to 31.5% operating margin in 2026, up from 29.5%, with free cash flow near $12.5 billion. The advertising business is on pace to roughly double to $3 billion, with the ad tier capturing over 60% of new sign-ups in ads markets and advertiser count up 70% YoY to over 4,000 clients. Valuation looks reasonable for a category leader at forward P/E of 29, with quarterly earnings growth of 86% YoY. Wall Street is overwhelmingly positive: 51 covering analysts, 37 are at Buy, 13 at Hold, and 1 at Sell with a $114.38 consensus target. Netflix resumed buybacks, repurchasing 13.5 million shares for $1.3 billion in Q1, leaving $6.8 billion authorized. The bear case rests on earnings quality and opportunity cost. The headline 82.77% net income jump leaned on a one-time Warner Bros. fee rather than core operations. Strip that out and the EPS miss is the real story. Q3 2025 also missed expectations on a Brazilian tax charge. Two ugly misses in three quarters from a company priced for precision is uncomfortable. Then there is the rest of the market. NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) have outpaced Netflix on share-price performance this year. Insiders have logged 119 recent transactions on a net selling basis at NFLX, while NVIDIA insiders are net buyers. Both sides have merit, but the case for action is thin. Netflix is growing, advertising is real, and 31.5% margins matter. The stock trades between its 50-day ($93.76) and 200-day ($104.66) moving averages, with a 52-week range of $75.01 to $134.12. That is the chart of a stock waiting for a catalyst rather than offering one. Upgrade conditions are concrete: two clean quarters without one-time noise, advertising tracking ahead of $3 billion, and Q2 margins landing on the 32.6% guide. Downgrade conditions are equally clear: another EPS miss, an ad-tier slowdown, or visible share losses to Disney, Amazon, or YouTube. Netflix currently trades at $92.37 with a market cap of $384.74 billion, a trailing P/E of 30, and a PEG of 1.8. The consensus target of $114.38 from 51 covering analysts implies roughly 23% upside, though targets are one input rather than a forecast. NFLX is down 1.59% YTD and 16.9% over the past year. Owning Netflix has cost investors versus several large-cap peers in the AI infrastructure trade. At $92.37, Netflix is a Hold. The bull case is intact, but the entry point is not generous enough to justify reallocation away from cheaper growth stories. A Buy thesis needs the forward P/E of 28 to compress as earnings catch up. With NVDA, AVGO, and beaten-down software names offering more growth per dollar of multiple, the risk-adjusted case for adding Netflix is weak. The Sell case is also a stretch. Netflix is profitable, growing double digits, expanding margins, returning capital, and scaling ad revenue. Dumping a category leader trading near its 50-day average because of one messy quarter feels reactive. Wait for a clean Q2, advertising scaling ahead of plan, and multiple compression toward 25 forward to tip this to a Buy. A miss on margin guide or ad revenue would tip it to a Sell. Until then, the risk-adjusted setup favors waiting. Omor Ibne Ehsan is a writer at 24/7 Wall St. He is a self-taught investor with a focus on growth and cyclical stocks that have strong fundamentals, value, and long-term potential. He also has an interest in high-risk, high-reward investments such as cryptocurrencies and penny stocks. Our top personal finance-related articles today. Your wallet will thank you later. Streaming has become a household necessity, with families carving out budgets even as economic headlines stay mixed. Title: Meta Platforms Just Made a Brilliant Move the Market Will Likely Read Wrong
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/04/30/meta-platforms-just-made-a-brilliant-move-the-market-will-likely-read-wrong/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T14:36:34Z
Full Content:
Investing Meta (META) beat Q1 earnings with $56.3B revenue (+33% YoY) and $10.44 EPS, but raised capex guidance to $125-$145B and plans a $25B 40-year debt deal at 6% coupon. Meta is locking in cheap, long-dated funding to bankroll AI infrastructure while advertising revenue accelerates from strong pricing and impression growth. The analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 stocks and Meta wasn't one of them. Get them here FREE. Sending You to Google News in 3 © PashaIgnatov / iStock via Getty Images Meta Platforms (NASDAQ: META | META Price Prediction) reported Q1 2026 results after the close on April 29, beating on both lines. Yet the stock opened down 9.79% this morning at $603.61. The market is fixating on a raised capex outlook and a planned debt deal. I think it is missing the bigger picture. Meta is locking in cheap, long-dated funding to press its AI advantage while the ad engine runs at full throttle. Revenue rose 33.08% year over year to $56.311 billion, the fastest top-line growth in nearly five years. Ad impressions climbed 19% and average price per ad rose 12%. Family daily active people reached 3.56 billion, up 4%. Operating income expanded 30.29% to $22.872 billion. I liked seeing pricing and impressions both up double digits. That is the signature of a healthy auction with durable demand. Two things. First, full-year capex guidance moved up to $125 billion to $145 billion from $115 billion to $135 billion, with roughly $135 billion headed largely toward AI infrastructure. Second, Meta confirmed plans to sell investment-grade debt, potentially up to $25 billion through a 40-year bond priced at 180 basis points over Treasuries. Reddit chatter shifted accordingly, with sentiment dropping from a bullish 72 pre-earnings to a neutral 40 to 54 range overnight. With the 10-year Treasury at 4.36%, a 1.8% spread implies an all-in coupon near 6%. Against FY2025 EBITDA of $104.5 billion and interest expense of just $1.165 billion, that is barely a rounding error on coverage. Meta is swapping near-term cash for 40-year capital to fund assets that will compound for a decade. The real risk lies in capex execution. Strip out the $3.13 tax tailwind and core EPS still beat decisively. That is what I would anchor on. CEO Mark Zuckerberg said Meta had “a milestone quarter with strong momentum across our apps and the release of our first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs” and added the company is “on track to deliver personal superintelligence to billions of people.” Management is doubling down. The capex hike is the cash version of that statement. Polymarket has 99.5% odds of a down close today, but 89.5% odds of $640 or higher in May. I would keep an eye on the bond pricing and Q2 ad pricing data. If the deal lands tight to 180 basis points and ad pricing holds, today’s selloff will look like a gift. After two decades of patrolling the dark corners of suburbia as a police officer, Rich Duprey hung up his badge and gun to begin writing full time about stocks and investing. For the past 20 years he’s been cruising the markets looking for companies to lock up as long-term holdings in a portfolio while writing extensively on the broad sectors of consumer goods, technology, and industrials. Because his experience isn’t from the typical financial analyst track, Rich is able to break down complex topics into understandable and useful action points for the average investor. His writings have appeared on The Motley Fool, InvestorPlace, Yahoo! Finance, and Money Morning. Title: Google shares hit all-time high on blowout earnings, market cap doubles to $4.4 trillion in just a year
URL: https://fortune.com/2026/04/30/google-shares-all-time-high-earnings/
Time Published: 2026-04-30T14:19:48Z
Full Content:
Google’s transition into the era of artificial intelligence continued to pay off for its corporate parent, Alphabet Inc., which on Wednesday announced another quarter of the stellar growth that helped to more than double its already lofty market value during the past year. Alphabet earned $62.6 billion, or $5.11 per share, during the January-March period, an 81% increase from the same time last year. Revenue climbed 22% from last year to $109.9 billion. Both numbers easily surpassed the analyst projections that steer investors. Alphabet’s stock price rose more than 6% in extended trading after the numbers came out, setting up the shares to hit a new high during Thursday’s regular session. The company’s market value currently stands at $4.4 trillion, up from $1.9 trillion just a year ago. That means it created roughly $250 billion in additional shareholder wealth in a single day. The stock market gains that Alphabet is producing are not being matched by other big AI spenders such as Microsoft and Facebook parent Meta Platforms, whose stock price plunged by about 6% in extended trading after disclosing an investment strategy being second guessed by investors. Meanwhile, Microsoft’s shares also dipped, despite posting quarterly results that topped analyst forecasts. Alphabet’s performance in the past quarter CEO Sundar Pichai to celebrate the huge bets that the company has been placing on AI technology during the past three years. Those investments, Pichai said, “are lighting up every part of the business.” As usual, digital ads fueled by Google’s dominant search engine propelled the growth as revenue from those operations shot up 16% from last year’s first quarter. It marked the fourth straight quarter that Google’s ad sales increased by more than 10% from the previous year. Google’s fastest growing division remains its Cloud division, which has been riding the AI boom to sell more products and services to corporate customers and government agencies such as the deal that it just struck with the U.S. military. Google Cloud’s revenue surged 63% from last year to $20 billion. That growth is a sign that Alphabet’s spending spree on AI is producing dividends so far, although investors continue to worry that the Mountain View, California, company and its Big Tech peers are pouring too much money into a still-nascent and unproven technology. Alphabet, though, is betting that it’s better to overspend on AI than being too stingy and risk behind left behind. In a previous quarterly update released in February, Alphabet disclosed that it’s earmarking $175 billion to $185 billion for capital expenditures this year that will largely be devoted to building AI data centers and other tools tied to the technology. In a reflection of management’s confidence in its strategy, Alphabet’s top finance executive Anat Ashkenazi told analysts on a conference call that this year’s capital expenditures may climb as high as $190 billion. And even if the spending runs that high, Askkenazi said it will “significantly increase” again next year. All of that would be on top of $91 billion in capital expenditures during 2025. “The key message is that Alphabet is no longer asking investors to underwrite AI spending on faith,” said Investing.com analyst Thomas Monteiro.

Title: Is Corning Stock A Buy At 50 Times Earnings?
URL: https://www.trefis.com/articles/597932/is-corning-stock-a-buy-at-50-times-earnings/2026-04-30
Time Published: 2026-04-30T14:05:24Z
Full Content:
Corning (GLW) reported Q1 2026 core sales of $4.35 billion, representing an 18% year-over-year increase. The core insight hiding beneath this headline beat is how entirely the AI-driven acceleration is offsetting the more modest growth currently seen in the company’s traditional Glass Innovations segment. Photo by Bru-nO on Pixabay The Optical Communications segment surged 36% year-over-year to $1.8 billion, driven by the structural demand for high-density AI data center architectures. The company secured a $6 billion multi-year agreement with Meta Platforms (META) alongside two similarly sized hyperscaler deals in Q1. Furthermore, the Solar segment recorded 80% year-over-year growth. This combined secular strength expanded core gross margins by 120 basis points to 39.1% and operating margins by 220 basis points to 20.2%. See how Corning’s growth and margins compare to those of its peers, including Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and Danaher (DHR). Corning’s core EPS rose 30% to $0.70, and management guided Q2 2026 sales to $4.6 billion. The AI infrastructure thesis is materializing exactly as projected. However, the Glass Innovations segment, which accounts for 32% of total revenue, stalled at just 1% year-over-year growth in Q1. Industry forecasts project global smartphone shipments will decline between 7% and 15% in 2026 due to component shortages and rising costs. A double-digit unit contraction will directly pressure high-margin Gorilla Glass volumes. Current market sentiment appears focused on the Optical surge, potentially underestimating the impact of near-term cyclical headwinds in mobile. Crucially, the market has already aggressively priced in this structural shift. Corning currently trades at 48 times its forward expected adjusted earnings. This is a severe expansion from its three-year average P/E multiple of approximately 21x. See how Corning’s stock valuation compares with its peers. Investors are valuing a capital-intensive, cyclical materials manufacturer at a premium typically reserved for asset-light technology companies. The fundamental asymmetry has deteriorated, narrowing the margin of safety for investors entering at these levels. The transition of Corning into a primary AI infrastructure enabler is verified by the Q1 data, but the current valuation demands flawless execution. If the optical segment growth normalizes below 25% or the smartphone market contraction exceeds a 10% decline, the 48x multiple will compress. The actionable strategy is to hold current positions and wait for the July IDC smartphone shipment data or an earnings-driven multiple contraction before allocating fresh capital. The $168 average of analysts’ price estimates implies only a 10% upside potential from here. Building long-term wealth requires a mechanism to manage these stock-specific risks while capturing the upside of the AI era. This objective is central to the Trefis High Quality Portfolio (HQ) strategy, which focuses on identifying companies with structural moats and high-integrity cash flows. The HQ strategy has outperformed its market benchmark since inception, delivering returns of over 105%. What Justifies Home Depot’s 23x Multiple? Is Acushnet Stock Optimizing Returns Through the Denominator Effect? Is Eli Lilly Stock Heading For A Fall? META Stock: The Math Behind The Upside
