This essay sums up current events in the AI world in a way that I can readily understand. Thanks. Some themes: First, when people think that AI is going to learn how to write, there are writers like Servaas Storm who lay that idea in a compost pile where it belongs. The wit, the jaunty style, the economy of language, the clearness of Storm’s thinking — AI is never going to be able to produce these hallmarks of good writing. Further, there are many highly amusing turns of phrase like “golden era of radical abundance” and “chasing its own tail” (not a new expression, but amusing when applied to a program that spends its time circling around data to grab). —Second, yet interpreting wit and appreciating the jaunty style requires empathy. It requires some experience and some depth of experience. None of this is possible in AI, which is mainly a program for compiling words into seemingly correct orders. As the Italian actor / satirist Paolo Camilli mentioned at a performance, AI will never work because it lacks empathy. He’s right. (Camilli also does a hilarious sendup of Sarah Jessica Parker as Carrie in Sex and the City, and one must have experience and empathy — not just a pile of words — to get the jokes.) —Third. I’m going to highlight this paragraph, because I don’t want these observations to get lost: A survey by software company WalkMe of 3750 executives and employees finds that, even though 81% of executives think their AI tools have “significantly improved productivity,” their workers are actually wasting eight hours per week cleaning up AI-slop, which is the equivalent of 51 work days a year. AI is not delivering. Why, that’s an argument for joining a union, so that a worker can get out from under management. Why? Productivity hadn’t gone down. The guys weren’t sitting at home in their boxer shorts eating boxes of Fannie Mae candy — people were doing their work. Yet managers have no purpose if they can’t drag the worker bees into the office and lecture them. The paragraph above makes a few things blatant: (1) every organization has too many managers who (2) don’t know what their own staffs do and (3) believe that their own presence is required, (4) when it isn’t, and (5) that what workers need is threats and pep talks. I am a translator, employed by an international fisheries agency (name withheld) for about 15 years now. I like to describe the impact of AI in my profession in the context of IT tool evolution since the Flintstones: In the beginning, there were no computers. There were translators and typists. The typists had to type the final versions delivered by translators into report text. Iteration 1: introduction of the first computers (late 1980s): the copy-paste function gets rid of the typists. One whole category of workers goes down the drain. Instant huge productivity gain. Iteration 2: introduction of translation memories (basically, databases with your previous translations and with whatever stuff you want to add; the software looks up matches and if something has been already translated it saves you translating and typing): significant productivity gain. Teams go from 3-4 per language to 2-3 per language. Not bad. In addition, very important gain in consistency. Very good in that regard, but that hardly counts in terms of productivity (quality and productivity are not necessarily correlated, are they?). Iteration 3: introduction of AI/automated translation (in our case, DeepL): significant but smaller productivity gain, with a serious consistency problem and longer-term human capacity issues. What I see at play here is the law of diminishing returns. And that is in an industry, translation, that AI was meant to completely automate. I am in my 50s and I don’t see a problem retiring as a translator, however long it takes me to pay off my mortgage. visca el pa i visca el vi: Are you seeing this mechanization across all languages? I do literary translation, rather than commercial translation. So I am not seeing as much mechanization. But then so much of Italian writing and literature never makes it into English anyway. Further, are you seeing it across all languages, or is the consolidation / mechanization mainly what goes into English? Which then, of course, consolidates the bad habit of depleted international business English as the default language. Or: If your target language is Catalan, is the process now just as mechanized? Or if the target is Basque (which may not even be allowed as a target…)? One advantage of the WWW has been to support minority languages with dictionaries, sites with grammar (I’ve used the Catalan language pages at the Generalitat de Catalunya), podcasts, vlogs (there are many interesting language teachers vlogging, like Portuguese with Leo and Podcast Italiano). https://www.piemonteis.com/ Note the list of other dictionaries. Thirty years ago, this much access to smaller languages wasn’t available. In short, I think machine-translation my be viable, but only in limited contexts, much as machine-generated subtitles on YuTube are good for following along, but regularly producers whoppers. Your advice, si us plau. My work is strictly Spanish-English. I do speak Catalan, but I don’t use it at work. Literary translation is a very different kind of work to mine. I have never done it and I suspect that I wouldn’t like the pay rate, although i love literature. And I would think that machine translation would be a very tricky tool to use to translate medieval poetry, although I can imagine uses for it for other texts. As to direccions, 95% of my work is English to Spanish, 5% the other way around. I haven’t perceived significant differences in quality depending on the direction. Re. minority languages, human-generated online Catalan corpus is large enough to provide for reasonable machine training, so I would assume that it works up to standard with any language that has a degree of institutional support (regional governments, universities…) that provides for volume and online access. My impression is that the quality issues correlate strongly with distance between languages. For example, translation from Indo-European languages to non Indo-European can be just a mess. (However, and contra my own argument, German or Scandinavian languages seem to machine-translate pretty badly. Perhaps it’s a subjective impression. I don’t know, I am purely guessing here). Also, the clearer the context, the better the quality. Cooking recipes, in that regard, are pretty well translated. Everything is clear in them, the machine does the job. Complex texts, forget about it. And then, you always need a human even to determinen which text is complex and which one you can fred the machine. That decision is Key to your productivity. Reviewing a bad machine translation can be much more onerous than doing the job yourself from scratch. This is getting long, but finally, as a gloomy Catalan, I have to comment on the world of possibilities that the internet opened up for minority languages: tools are nothing without speakers. We have a fantastic profusion of tools, extraordinary resources, but the path of minority languages may have been best described by linguist Louis-Jean Calvet: a solar system with a hierarchy where English reigns as the sun and lords over, contaminates and absorbs the other major languages, that, in turn, lord over, contaminate and absorb smaller languages. My pessimistic view is that the online sphere works towards linguistic homogeneization and vastly outdoes anything that the availability of minority language resources would have to offer. Anyway, going back to machine translation, it is a tool, it has its pros and cons, and I am not concerned that it is going to ever do my job. It will change it, yes, but never replace me. My concern is that my next boss may one day listen to an AI guru on YouTube, high on something, and think that that shit works so well that he doesn’t need me anymore. By the time he finds out how mistaken he was, I would have lost my job. That is my only AI problem. You might enjoy David Bellos’ book “Is that a fish in your ear?” about translation. There is a chapter touching on what I guess I’ll call internet translation, focusing on Google Translate. Interestingly, it predated the current “””AI”””/LLM froth (‘AI’ is not in the index) but in describing how Google Translate works, he describes how what we now lazily call “AI” works exactly, ie that GT trains on vast datasets of work of translators going back a long time, and searches that work for likely meanings, and predicts meaning based on that data, usually (but not always!) successfully. Bellos is a literary translator but he touches on all facets of translation in the book including technical, diplomatic, &c Sorry to hear about that. Translations can be a tricky business and an AI may not understand the intent behind those words but will just copy-paste what it has learned to do in the past. Minister announces withdrawal of draft AI Policy SAnews With respect to the article about the courts linked in Teapot Alert #4, two thirds of civil litigants in state courts are pro se, most probably due to financial necessity. This area comprises things like landlord/tenant disputes and debt collection, where often workers are brought to court by some powerful entity against which they have little power to defend themselves. Suing companies, landlords, or the government effectively, if it can be done at all, requires counsel. AI used by pro se litigants clogging up the courts is an interesting evolution of the phenomenon represented by “sovereign citizens”, namely the class gap in expertise in dealing with languages created to obscure and mystify the workings of power. Some see the answer to this in a “civil Gideon” which would grant the right to an attorney to litigants in civil cases, but I’m skeptical. Hopefully the AI bubble popping provides us all some relief. One note from the field. “A closer look at the one-third of U.S. occupations with high AI-exposure does suggest that AI-tools will not replace most jobs but reshape them. Anthropic’s AI-exposure metric has no predictive power concerning expected job growth by occupations, based on the BLS-projections of job growth during the coming decade. To illustrate, not a single radiologist lost her job because of AI. Employment for structural biologists has, so far, not declined due to AI-protein folding. What happened is that structural biologists now spend less time on determining protein structures and more time on interpreting, validating and using them. In fact, AI tools (like AlphaFold) created new jobs for computational structural biologists, bio-informatics experts, cryo-electron microscopists, and pharma biologists.” Yes. And why has AlphaFold been so successful? Because the training set is as correct and complete as any training set imaginable: All of the three-dimensional protein structures in the PDB, which have been determined and vetted over the past 70 years. Now, instead of spending years determining a new structure, biologists of all kinds can use AlphaFold intelligently and intentionally to go beyond that and take on larger projects, such as the coevolution of proteins in multicomponent structures that make cells what they are. This is my dream, anyway, hopefully as a minor contributor somewhere down the road. It must also be remembered that AlphaFold structures must be confirmed experimentally, one way or another, by several complementary approaches. Points 3 and 6 seem kind of related to me. I suspect that the loss of that tax break caused more software job losses than most people realize. So, was it really paid for with Hannah’s money? Regarding #1, hallucination is not unique to AIs (talk to an anti-vaxxer sometime). What is unique to AIs is their inability to learn and improve their ‘mental models’ over time. If you can convince an anti-vaxxer to listen to you for long enough, and you reason with them, you have a chance of snapping them out of their hallucinations and back to a more reality-based view of the world – and, once done, that state will persist for the long term. You can’t do this with LLMs. It might look like you can – you can correct them, or even ask them to critique and correct themselves, and they will appear to listen and to do so successfully. But they aren’t really – they are just imitating what our hypothetical reformed anti-vaxxer might say in that kind of situation. Because they have no mental model of the world, or capability for abstraction or long term memory, they aren’t capable of durable change the way humans are. It’s one of the things that makes AIs frustrating to use. You can prompt them regarding their mistakes, and they will correctly diagnose them and describe how they can be mitigated or avoided, and then turn around and do the exact same thing over again. China’s social credit system is a demonstration of a way for the western oligarchies to follow. And, of course, it may well fall into the bankster category of too big to fail when the fan and excrement connect. The American model for AI is inherently flawed and is too costly, consumes too many resources, and does not deliver proper output—much like industry in general in the West. Of course AI is interesting, but so far, more like a clown-show is interesting. The Chinese have a fundamentally different approach, one that is not based on the finance capital reality. Socialist AI works! Your email address will not be published. Required fields are marked * Comment * Name * Email * SUBSCRIPTIONS

Title: Met Gala insiders leak 'disturbing' drama plaguing first event bankrolled by Jeff Bezos and Lauren Sanchez: 'Slap in the face' break with tradition... Anna Wintour's new role... and A-lister who backed out
URL: https://www.dailymail.com/lifestyle/article-15763521/met-gala-jeff-bezos-lauren-sanchez-anna-wintour-meryl-streep.html
Time Published: 2026-04-29T00:03:12Z
Full Content:
By CADY LANG - US FEATURES WRITER Updated: 09:40 EDT, 29 April 2026 89 View comments Usually, it's all about the fashion. But, as A-list celebrities and society power players ascend the steps of the Metropolitan Museum of Art for fashion's biggest night on Monday, for once it will be something - or perhaps more pertinently someone - else garnering all the attention. Gone is the chatter about the exclusively tight guest list over which Conde Nast maven Anna Wintour has reigned supreme as the chairwoman of the Costume Institute benefit, known colloquially as the Met Gala, for over 30 years - because gone is that exclusivity, judging by who will be greeting guests at the top. Instead, insiders have told the Daily Mail, all eyes will be fixed on the glittering gala's controversial lead sponsors and honorary co-chairs, Amazon founder and tech billionaire Jeff Bezos and his wife, Lauren Sanchez Bezos. A fashion industry insider told the Daily Mail: 'It's, quite frankly, very hurtful and very disturbing for [the Bezoses] to be the face of this year's Met Gala, something that is really meaningful to a lot of people and to such a highly regarded and highly respected institution. 'It feels like a real slap in the face to a lot of people.' Indeed, the Daily Mail has learned that, the Bezoses' involvement has cast a shadow over the ritzy event, dubbed the Oscars of fashion for its unabashed display of opulent glamour. According to our source, at least one A-list heavyweight has pulled out of Met Gala programming as a result. The Daily Mail has been told that Meryl Streep - who appeared on the May 2026 Vogue cover alongside Wintour for The Devil Wears Prada 2 promotion - was rumored to have been one of the Met Gala's co-chairs this year, but allegedly declined to do so because of the Bezoses' bankrolling and presiding over the party as guests of honor. Insiders have told the Daily Mail that all eyes will be fixed on the glittering gala's controversial lead sponsors and honorary co-chairs, Amazon founder and tech billionaire Jeff Bezos and his wife, Lauren Sanchez Bezos Streep appeared on a Vogue cover alongside Wintour for The Devil Wears Prada 2 promotion The Daily Mail has been told that Meryl Streep was rumored to have been one of the Met Gala's co-chairs this year, but allegedly declined to do so because of the Bezoses Representatives for Streep did not respond to the Daily Mail's request for comment. Backlash against the hyper-affluent couple has been building since the November announcement. For some, Bezos's apparent alignment with the Trump administration is simply not a good look. Online commentators have gone as far as to label the event the 'Jeff Bezos Nazi Met Gala' on social media and anti-billionaire activists have plastered New York City subway stations and streets with posters calling for a boycott of fashion's biggest night. Internal drama is just as rampant, the fashion industry insider said, as there's been dissent quietly brewing among those involved with planning the annual event - a fundraiser for the museum's fashion collection. They explained: 'The fashion industry and the people who work in it are by and large, very liberal. Anna has always publicly supported Democratic candidates for president and Vogue endorsed Hillary Clinton and Kamala Harris. Even the politicians that we've seen attend the Met Gala over the years have been overwhelmingly liberal. 'To have a couple that aligns themselves with and have contributed a million dollars to inaugurating a president who has sought to eviscerate DEI, dismantle Roe v Wade, disenfranchise Black voters and crack down on immigration in a way that is so inhumane and horrific, rubs a lot of people the wrong way.' Not that the couple aren't used to controversy. Their lavish June 2025 Venice wedding was met with protests, while the Bezos-owned privatized space endeavor, Blue Origin, which saw Sanchez Bezos go to space with an all-female crew in April 2025, was derided as 'gluttonous.' Bezos, who has a net worth of $259 billion, faced additional scrutiny for his Met Gala sponsorship amid the gutting layoffs that took place in early 2026 at the Washington Post and Amazon and saw over 16,300 people across both companies lose their jobs. For many the Bezoses' underwriting of the Met Gala is a less than subtle attempt to buy their way into the elite circles of the notoriously exclusive fashion industry. In recent years, their desire to be accepted within these upper echelons has been apparent. At couture week in Paris earlier this year, they were spotted cozying up to Wintour and Dior's creative director, Jonathan Anderson, as well as with a Vogue digital cover story in 2025 that showcased Sanchez Bezos's Dolce & Gabbana wedding dress. To be the main sponsors of the exorbitantly expensive Met Gala, where individual tickets clock in at $100,000 and tables go for a whopping $350,000, is perhaps the ultimate flex of their immense financial power. According to our fashion source, the Bezoses' roles as honorary co-chairs of the Met Gala also signals that Wintour has given them her blessing and that others in the fashion industry will follow suit - though many of them may harbor deep reservations in private. 'The Met Gala is the most exclusive, coveted and sought after invitation on the fashion social calendar, so to have top billing on that invite as the sponsor is absolutely an embrace and a stamp of approval,' the source said. 'Obviously, the Met Gala is always over-the-top and wealth and grandeur is always on display, but there are two of the richest people to ever exist in the world who will be hosting it. When we're in the midst of a war and there are so many people struggling to have their basic needs met, it's not necessarily the image everyone wants to see.' Anna Wintour has reigned supreme as the chairwoman of the Costume Institute benefit, known colloquially as the Met Gala, for over 30 years For many, the Bezoses' underwriting of the Met Gala is a less than subtle attempt to buy their way into the elite circles of the notoriously exclusive fashion industry The Bezoses' desire to be accepted within these upper fashion echelons has been apparent, especially during couture week in Paris earlier this year Amy Odell, the author of Anna: The Biography, said that the couple may be facing additional scrutiny for their outsized fiscal role. 'The Met Gala is not normally primarily sponsored by individual donors,' Odell told the Daily Mail. 'It's normally brands that take on that task to foot the bill. For the leading sponsors to be two individuals is unusual. And they're not popular, so that's been very controversial as for why she [Anna] went to them. Obviously, their wealth is so immense that whatever they're spending is probably like pocket change to them.' While the current political and economic climate is fueling increased scrutiny of the Bezoses' financial backing, Odell said that the same economic downturn may be the very reason why Wintour decided to tap the Bezoses in the first place. 'Why did she go to them? I can only imagine that brands are not ponying up as much - if you look at luxury brands like Kering and LVMH, fashion has been in a post-pandemic slump, so that could be why,' Odell said. The fashion insider agreed: 'It's much easier to wrap your head around a luxury brand that has been around for decades having the resources to sponsor an event like the Met Gala. But to reckon with the idea that two individuals can do it during a time like now, I think a lot of people question the ethics of that kind of wealth.' There's also been speculation that the changes are due to Wintour stepping down from her role as the editorial director of Vogue in late 2025 and elevating Chloe Malle to the top with the new title, Head of Editorial Content. However, Odell, who writes about Wintour often in her newsletter, Back Row, said that Wintour's influence, especially when it comes to the Met Gala, has not diminished in the slightest - meaning that her selection of Bezoses as the lead sponsors was intentional and considered. Odell said: 'The misconception that many of us have made in observing her new position at Conde Nast is assuming that it's a step back, when, in reality, I think she may have been looking at it as passing off what no longer suited her. 'Running a media property, where you're hustling pageviews and coming up with events and doing social media content and looking at reports - my hunch is that she didn't really want to do that anymore, so she assigned that to somebody else. It's worth remembering that when Anna announced her change in role in a Vogue article, it stated that it would allow Anna to focus more on other projects like the Met Gala.' The inside source also hinted that Malle wouldn't be opposing Wintour's decisions, especially when it came to the Bezoses. In fact, the fashion insider shared that Malle, who wrote both of the glowing profiles of Sanchez Bezos in Vogue, had talked in the Vogue offices about her chummy relationship with her. 'Obviously, their wealth is so immense that whatever they're spending is probably like pocket change to them,' Odell said of the Bezoses, pictured here with Wintour front row at the Tom Ford fall/winter runway show in February 2020 The inside source also hinted that Chloe Malle (pictured to the right of Wintour) wouldn't be opposing Wintour's decisions, especially when it came to the Bezoses While Wintour has been taken to task by critics and the peanut gallery alike for making the Bezoses the lead sponsors, Odell said the outraged reactions may be more indicative as a sign of the times than of her actual co-signing of the billionaire couple themselves. 'The sociopolitical landscape is really different today. Billionaires are not well-liked,' Odell said. 'Income inequality is so stark and it's become a very political issue. Anna wasn't up against the same climate in 2015.' Despite her continued influence, Wintour has still faced flack in some of fashion's inner circles. The inside source said that platforming the Bezoses is 'hypocritical,' given Wintour's longtime soft power moves for Democratic politics and as her stance as a proponent of DEI, especially in the wake of last year's Met Gala theme honoring Black style. They said: 'To have the 2025 Met Gala that was a celebration of the Black dandy, which was groundbreaking and historic to then, a year later, have the host be a couple who publicly supports an administration that is actively rolling back DEI initiatives and civil rights, is so disrespectful and distasteful. It makes last year's Met Gala seem extremely performative.' Representatives for Vogue and the Bezoses did not respond to the Daily Mail's request for comment. Odell, however, said that Wintour may appear to be less concerned with political affiliations than she is with her proximity to who's currently in power. She points out that a source for her book on Wintour, Anna: The Biography, said that the longtime editor was 'attracted to people who are powerful,' a currency that's always en vogue. Title: Bitcoin rally falters as AI industry weakens and CLARITY Act approval odds fall
URL: https://cointelegraph.com/markets/bitcoin-rally-falters-as-ai-industry-weakens-and-clarity-act-approval-odds-fall
Time Published: 2026-04-28T23:47:37Z
Full Content:
Bitcoin’s dip below $76,000 was driven by an AI sector sell-off and investors’ worries about slowed progress in the CLARITY Act negotiations. Key takeaways: Bitcoin (BTC) retreated below $76,000 on Tuesday, erasing gains from the prior week. This movement followed a 1% decline in the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 Index after OpenAI reported a shortfall in its revenue and user growth targets. While the AI industry may be a factor in Bitcoin’s decline, crypto market regulations and macroeconomic indicators are also contributing. Nasdaq 100 futures (left) vs. Bitcoin/USD (right). Source: TradingView The Nasdaq 100 Index traded down 1% on Tuesday as AI infrastructure companies displayed weakness following a Wall Street Journal report that ChatGPT developer OpenAI announced lackluster sales and user metrics for 2025. Shares of Nvidia (NVDA US), Oracle (ORCL US), and CoreWeave (CRWV US) fell more than 2%. The downturn in technology stocks can also be attributed to routine profit-taking, as the Nasdaq 100 Index reached an all-time high on Monday. Traders adopted a more cautious approach ahead of quarterly earnings reports from Microsoft (MSFT US), Google (GOOGL US), Amazon (AMZN US), and Meta (META US) on Wednesday, with Apple (AAPL US) following on Thursday. Brent crude oil spiked to $110 as US-Iran negotiations stalled over nuclear enrichment, threatening traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, China’s major cities experienced significant declines in real estate, with existing home prices dropping 7.4%. In the US, although the S&P Case-Shiller Index rose 0.3%, over half the country saw price decreases. In addition to the current macroeconomic factors, Bitcoin traders are skeptical about stalled progress on the CLARITY Act. Despite the pro-crypto stance from the Trump administration, the expected advancements have not fully materialized. If the market perception of crypto regulation improves, it could serve as the necessary catalyst to drive institutional demand back into Bitcoin. Related: Acting AG Todd Blanche confirms ‘code is not a crime’ in DOJ pivot Odds of crypto market structure legislation approval by 2027. Source: Kalshi Traders are currently pricing in lower odds of the CLARITY Act's approval. This crypto market structure bill cleared the House of Representatives in July 2025 but has since stalled in the Senate Banking Committee. While it is impossible to pinpoint the exact drivers behind the Bitcoin price correction to $76,000, the lack of momentum in US-Iran negotiations, weakness in real estate markets, and negative regulatory pressure have likely undermined investor confidence. These factors, alongside the downturn in technology stocks on Tuesday, have created a challenging environment for Bitcoin. Cointelegraph is committed to providing independent, high-quality journalism across the crypto, blockchain, AI, and fintech industries. All news, reviews, and analyses are produced with full journalistic independence and integrity. For more details on our standards and processes, please read our Editorial Policy.

Title: The Semiconductor Bubble Goes Nuclear
URL: https://dailyreckoning.com/the-semiconductor-bubble-goes-nuclear/
Time Published: 2026-04-28T22:00:42Z
Full Content:
By Adam Sharp PostedApril 28, 2026 In the past year, software stocks have gotten crushed while semiconductors soared. In the chart below, we can see that semiconductor stocks (blue) are walloping software (red) in 2026. Source: Opening Bell Daily Software stocks include companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Adobe (ADBE), and Figma (FIG). Fears over AI disruption, combined with silly-high valuations, have caused investors to flee the sector. Meanwhile, all the money is being made in semiconductors (microchips). Companies like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Intel (INTC), Qualcomm (QCOM), are absolutely crushing it. Semiconductor firms have been the prime beneficiaries of the AI boom. They are the ones selling picks and shovels in this gold rush. The hottest semi stock lately is Intel (INTC). It has now risen from an August 2025 low of $19 to its current price of $83.54. For decades, the company was a dog. But ever since the U.S. government invested in the company back in August of 2025, it’s been on a record-breaking run. After such incredible performance, semiconductor stocks are getting extremely expensive. Take a look at the chart below, which shows the SOX semiconductor index’s P/E ratio (blue, left side) and price/book ratio (white, right side). Source: Bloomberg As you can see, semiconductor companies on average are trading at a 60 P/E. And a price/book ratio of 10. Here’s an easy way to think about P/E ratios. At a P/E of 60, if earnings stayed the same, it would take 60 years for semiconductor stocks to generate profits equal to their market value. That’s a long time. Justifying that valuation means counting on decades of high growth ahead. Could it happen? I suppose anything is possible. But those are some very bubbly numbers. Reminiscent of the dotcom bubble. Below is a chart comparing the weight of semiconductor stocks over the past 50+ years. As you can see, chip companies currently make up around 14% of the total stock market value. That’s double what they peaked at in 2000. Semiconductor stocks look highly overbought at this stage. They’re now more than 40% above the 200-day moving average. In 2000, it briefly got over 100% of its 200-day moving average. So I suppose we could still have further to go. But historically, these types of moves end the same way. In a crash. In 2000, Intel shares reached $75. By 2002, the stock had fallen to a low of around $12. An 83% collapse. Intel just recently surpassed its 2000 peak, 26 years later. So if you’ve got a lot of exposure to semiconductor stocks, like Nvidia, Intel, or Qualcomm, now might be a good time to take some profits. Honestly, Nvidia looks like the cheapest of the bunch. But still, when this bubble pops, it’ll undoubtedly take a big hit. I’m confident we’ll get a better chance to buy semiconductor/chip stocks sometime in the next 3 years. Yes, AI is going to play a big role in the future of tech. And that will require a lot of semiconductors. But AI efficiency is improving by leaps and bounds, and eventually chip production will catch up with demand. And the bubble will pop. Be sure to take some profits before that happens.
--------------------------------------------------

Title: Google Faces a Rising Bar to Impress Investors With Its Results
URL: https://www.investopedia.com/google-faces-a-rising-bar-to-impress-investors-with-its-results-11960342
Time Published: 2026-04-28T21:38:46Z
Full Content:
Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise. Google parent Alphabet could face an increasingly difficult bar to clear to impress investors with its quarterly results tomorrow. Expectations are rising ahead of what analysts are projecting could be a blockbuster report from the tech giant, after a series of new partnerships and wins for its AI models that have boosted enthusiasm for the shares, amid a broader tech rally and improving sentiment around the AI industry. Shares of Alphabet (GOOGL) have rallied to record highs in recent weeks, with the shares up 11% since the start of the year compared to a 4% rise for the S&P 500. Among the the Magnificent Seven, it trails only Nvidia (NVDA) and Amazon (AMZN), which are up about 14% and 13%, respectively. Amazon is also due to release its earnings Wednesday, while Nvidia is set to report next month. Earnings from Alphabet and several other tech giants this week could provide the catalyst for the AI trade to keep rallying, or pull back if the results don't meet investors' growing expectations. Alphabet is now seen reporting revenue of $107.02 billion for the first quarter, a figure that has climbed in the days leading up to the report, and would represent a nearly 19% rise from a year ago. Its earnings per share are projected to fall 3 cents to $2.73 amid growing AI spending. Analysts have said they will be watching closely for indications that demand is improving for Google's Gemini AI products, along with growth in the company's advertising revenue and Google Cloud's computing services. Alphabet shares were little changed Tuesday, on a day when several AI-related stocks slipped following a report that OpenAI missed some revenue and user growth targets. Title: Custom Truck One Source Q1 Earnings Call Highlights
URL: https://www.marketbeat.com/instant-alerts/custom-truck-one-source-q1-earnings-call-highlights-2026-04-28/?utm_source=yahoofinance&amp;utm_medium=yahoofinance
Time Published: 2026-04-28T20:31:38Z
Full Content:
Custom Truck One Source NYSE: CTOS reported what management described as a strong start to fiscal 2026, posting record first-quarter revenue and higher profitability driven largely by continued momentum in its rental business serving transmission and distribution (T&D) utility markets. Get CTOS alerts:Sign UpFirst-quarter results and segment reporting changes CEO Ryan McMonagle said the company generated first-quarter revenue of $462 million and Adjusted EBITDA of $98 million, representing year-over-year increases of 9% and 33%, respectively. CFO Christopher Eperjesy attributed the performance to “stronger operating performance across the business and improved rental fundamentals, particularly in our T&D end markets.” Heading into 2026, Custom Truck implemented a new segment reporting structure: Specialty Equipment Rentals (SER) and Specialty Truck Equipment and Manufacturing (STEM). Investor Relations head Brian Perman and Eperjesy cautioned that prior-period segment disclosures are not fully comparable because 2025 segment results in the earnings materials did not include margin on inter-segment sales, while 2026 results do. Management directed investors to reconciliation and illustrative materials in the investor presentation appendices. SER: High utilization, growing OEC on rent, and improving yield Management highlighted SER as the key driver in the quarter, citing improving rental KPIs that carried over from 2025. McMonagle said rental fleet utilization averaged 81.4% in the quarter, up 370 basis points from the year-ago period. He also pointed to average original equipment cost (OEC) on rent of $1.34 billion, up 12% year over year, and said both utilization and OEC on rent were trending above first-quarter averages so far in the second quarter. Eperjesy said SER third-party revenue (excluding inter-segment sales) rose 16% year over year to $194 million, driven by “strong double-digit growth in both rental revenue and rental equipment sales activity.” SER segment Adjusted EBITDA increased 23% to $105 million, with an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 51.5%, up more than 415 basis points from the prior year’s first quarter. On-rent yield in SER was 38.9%, and Eperjesy said the metric remained within the company’s “targeted upper 30s-low 40% range.” McMonagle said yield benefited from both pricing and mix, noting the company took a roughly 5% rental price increase in December and has seen transmission work “coming on very strong,” which he said carries higher yield than distribution due to equipment type. He added that price changes take time to fully flow through because increases occur as new equipment goes out on rent. The company ended the quarter with total OEC of about $1.66 billion, which McMonagle called the highest quarter-end level in its history. Eperjesy said net rental CapEx in Q1 was more than $49 million and that fleet age ended the quarter “just under 3 years,” consistent with the company’s plan to reduce maintenance CapEx and “age the fleet somewhat this year.” STEM: Backlog growth, cost actions, and stable pricing conditions In STEM, Eperjesy reported first-quarter third-party revenue of $268 million, up 5% year over year. The increase included equipment sales growth of more than 4% and parts sales and service revenue growth of almost 17%. STEM segment Adjusted EBITDA was $33 million with a 9% margin, which management said reflected significant cost-out and productivity improvements led by the production team, while also noting the impact of inter-segment margin accounting changes between 2025 and 2026. McMonagle said STEM revenue (excluding sales to SER) rose 5% year over year and that gross margin expanded on productivity improvements. New sales order backlog ended the quarter at $411 million, up more than $76 million sequentially, and Eperjesy said it had grown further to more than $425 million so far in the second quarter. McMonagle said the “far majority” of the backlog is expected to deliver in 2026. Asked about backlog composition, McMonagle said the largest pickup came from local and regional customers, with strength in “utility and forestry” within that cohort. He added the company has “still seen less of a pickup” in infrastructure-related categories and has not yet seen a significant backlog increase in waste and dump truck segments. On new equipment pricing, Eperjesy said conditions were “more stable” than a year ago, though he acknowledged some pressure remains and said productivity and cost initiatives have helped offset it. Guidance raised for EBITDA, revenue outlook reaffirmed Custom Truck reaffirmed its full-year 2026 revenue outlook while raising its Adjusted EBITDA guidance. McMonagle said the company continues to expect consolidated revenue of $2.005 billion to $2.12 billion. However, he raised both ends of the Adjusted EBITDA range to $415 million to $440 million. Eperjesy said the EBITDA increase reflects rental outperformance and operating execution, “and not so much…anything on the top line in terms of a more aggressive top-line assumption.” In response to questions about why revenue guidance was unchanged despite strong SER performance, management said the outperformance versus the prior year has been more notable on margins than on revenue, supporting a higher EBITDA outlook while keeping the revenue range intact for now. For segment revenue guidance, the company reiterated: SER revenue: $835 million to $870 million STEM revenue: $1.58 billion to $1.655 billion Eperjesy also noted that overall STEM sales, including inter-segment sales, are expected to be “flat to slightly down” due solely to the expected reduction in SER maintenance rental CapEx. He added that despite a tough comparison in the second quarter of 2025 due to near-record new equipment sales, the company still expects year-over-year growth in Adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter of 2026 given current trends. Balance sheet, cash flow priorities, and tariff/EPA considerations Eperjesy said the company ended the quarter with net debt of $1.65 billion and last-twelve-month Adjusted EBITDA of more than $408 million, resulting in net leverage “slightly more than 4 times.” He said leverage improved about 30 basis points sequentially and about 80 basis points versus the year-ago quarter. Availability under the asset-based lending facility was $257 million as of March 31, and he said the company could potentially access additional availability by upsizing the facility based on the borrowing base. Free cash flow generation and deleveraging remain key priorities, according to management. Eperjesy said the company expects more than $50 million of levered free cash flow in 2026 and aims to reduce net leverage to “meaningfully below 4 times” by fiscal year-end, while progressing toward a 3x target in 2027. In response to a question from JPMorgan’s Tami Zakaria, Eperjesy said the expected levered free cash flow “would all be used to pay down debt.” On working capital, Eperjesy said inventory was “probably closer to seven and a half months right now” and reiterated the goal of getting below six months on hand. He said the company expects to take “north of $100 million year-over-year out of inventory” as part of its 2026 working capital initiative, while noting most inventory is floor planned and that a $100 million inventory reduction typically equates to roughly $20 million of cash benefit. He said working capital could contribute $30 million to $40 million toward the company’s free cash flow expectation for the year. During Q&A, McMonagle addressed potential impacts from tariffs and the EPA’s 2027 emissions standards. On Section 232 tariffs, he said the company has “inventory on the ground” and is seeing “a little bit of tariff exposure on some of our bodies,” but added the team has managed it and that OEM conversations are increasingly centered on ordering ahead of 2027. Regarding EPA 2027, he said the company’s fleet age, inventory position, and relationships with chassis OEM partners and dealers leave it “very well-positioned,” while the company monitors remaining open questions around warranty and final EPA rulings. Management also fielded questions about potential data center-related pushback affecting T&D demand. McMonagle said the company is still seeing strong demand from customers and that “specific noise around data center doesn’t seem to be impacting our customers and the work that they are planning to start” over coming quarters and years.About Custom Truck One Source NYSE: CTOSCustom Truck One Source, Inc NYSE: CTOS is a North American provider of specialty rental equipment, parts and services. The company's fleet encompasses a wide range of assets, including cranes, aerial work platforms, trench safety and shoring equipment, fluid management solutions, generators and other industrial machinery. Customers rely on Custom Truck One Source to support projects in construction, energy, telecommunications, industrial manufacturing, municipalities and large-scale events. Headquartered in Plano, Texas, Custom Truck One Source has expanded through a combination of organic growth and strategic acquisitions to establish a network of more than 140 branch locations across the United States and Canada.Further ReadingFive stocks we like better than Custom Truck One SourceThis instant news alert was generated by narrative science technology and financial data from MarketBeat in order to provide readers with the fastest reporting and unbiased coverage. CFO Christopher Eperjesy attributed the performance to “stronger operating performance across the business and improved rental fundamentals, particularly in our T&D end markets.” Heading into 2026, Custom Truck implemented a new segment reporting structure: Specialty Equipment Rentals (SER) and Specialty Truck Equipment and Manufacturing (STEM). Investor Relations head Brian Perman and Eperjesy cautioned that prior-period segment disclosures are not fully comparable because 2025 segment results in the earnings materials did not include margin on inter-segment sales, while 2026 results do. Management directed investors to reconciliation and illustrative materials in the investor presentation appendices. SER: High utilization, growing OEC on rent, and improving yield Management highlighted SER as the key driver in the quarter, citing improving rental KPIs that carried over from 2025. McMonagle said rental fleet utilization averaged 81.4% in the quarter, up 370 basis points from the year-ago period. He also pointed to average original equipment cost (OEC) on rent of $1.34 billion, up 12% year over year, and said both utilization and OEC on rent were trending above first-quarter averages so far in the second quarter. Eperjesy said SER third-party revenue (excluding inter-segment sales) rose 16% year over year to $194 million, driven by “strong double-digit growth in both rental revenue and rental equipment sales activity.” SER segment Adjusted EBITDA increased 23% to $105 million, with an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 51.5%, up more than 415 basis points from the prior year’s first quarter. On-rent yield in SER was 38.9%, and Eperjesy said the metric remained within the company’s “targeted upper 30s-low 40% range.” McMonagle said yield benefited from both pricing and mix, noting the company took a roughly 5% rental price increase in December and has seen transmission work “coming on very strong,” which he said carries higher yield than distribution due to equipment type. He added that price changes take time to fully flow through because increases occur as new equipment goes out on rent. The company ended the quarter with total OEC of about $1.66 billion, which McMonagle called the highest quarter-end level in its history. Eperjesy said net rental CapEx in Q1 was more than $49 million and that fleet age ended the quarter “just under 3 years,” consistent with the company’s plan to reduce maintenance CapEx and “age the fleet somewhat this year.” STEM: Backlog growth, cost actions, and stable pricing conditions In STEM, Eperjesy reported first-quarter third-party revenue of $268 million, up 5% year over year. The increase included equipment sales growth of more than 4% and parts sales and service revenue growth of almost 17%. STEM segment Adjusted EBITDA was $33 million with a 9% margin, which management said reflected significant cost-out and productivity improvements led by the production team, while also noting the impact of inter-segment margin accounting changes between 2025 and 2026. McMonagle said STEM revenue (excluding sales to SER) rose 5% year over year and that gross margin expanded on productivity improvements. New sales order backlog ended the quarter at $411 million, up more than $76 million sequentially, and Eperjesy said it had grown further to more than $425 million so far in the second quarter. McMonagle said the “far majority” of the backlog is expected to deliver in 2026. Asked about backlog composition, McMonagle said the largest pickup came from local and regional customers, with strength in “utility and forestry” within that cohort. He added the company has “still seen less of a pickup” in infrastructure-related categories and has not yet seen a significant backlog increase in waste and dump truck segments. On new equipment pricing, Eperjesy said conditions were “more stable” than a year ago, though he acknowledged some pressure remains and said productivity and cost initiatives have helped offset it. Guidance raised for EBITDA, revenue outlook reaffirmed Custom Truck reaffirmed its full-year 2026 revenue outlook while raising its Adjusted EBITDA guidance. McMonagle said the company continues to expect consolidated revenue of $2.005 billion to $2.12 billion. However, he raised both ends of the Adjusted EBITDA range to $415 million to $440 million. Eperjesy said the EBITDA increase reflects rental outperformance and operating execution, “and not so much…anything on the top line in terms of a more aggressive top-line assumption.” In response to questions about why revenue guidance was unchanged despite strong SER performance, management said the outperformance versus the prior year has been more notable on margins than on revenue, supporting a higher EBITDA outlook while keeping the revenue range intact for now. For segment revenue guidance, the company reiterated: SER revenue: $835 million to $870 million STEM revenue: $1.58 billion to $1.655 billion Eperjesy also noted that overall STEM sales, including inter-segment sales, are expected to be “flat to slightly down” due solely to the expected reduction in SER maintenance rental CapEx. He added that despite a tough comparison in the second quarter of 2025 due to near-record new equipment sales, the company still expects year-over-year growth in Adjusted EBITDA in the second quarter of 2026 given current trends. Balance sheet, cash flow priorities, and tariff/EPA considerations Eperjesy said the company ended the quarter with net debt of $1.65 billion and last-twelve-month Adjusted EBITDA of more than $408 million, resulting in net leverage “slightly more than 4 times.” He said leverage improved about 30 basis points sequentially and about 80 basis points versus the year-ago quarter. Availability under the asset-based lending facility was $257 million as of March 31, and he said the company could potentially access additional availability by upsizing the facility based on the borrowing base. Free cash flow generation and deleveraging remain key priorities, according to management. Eperjesy said the company expects more than $50 million of levered free cash flow in 2026 and aims to reduce net leverage to “meaningfully below 4 times” by fiscal year-end, while progressing toward a 3x target in 2027. In response to a question from JPMorgan’s Tami Zakaria, Eperjesy said the expected levered free cash flow “would all be used to pay down debt.” On working capital, Eperjesy said inventory was “probably closer to seven and a half months right now” and reiterated the goal of getting below six months on hand. He said the company expects to take “north of $100 million year-over-year out of inventory” as part of its 2026 working capital initiative, while noting most inventory is floor planned and that a $100 million inventory reduction typically equates to roughly $20 million of cash benefit. He said working capital could contribute $30 million to $40 million toward the company’s free cash flow expectation for the year. During Q&A, McMonagle addressed potential impacts from tariffs and the EPA’s 2027 emissions standards. On Section 232 tariffs, he said the company has “inventory on the ground” and is seeing “a little bit of tariff exposure on some of our bodies,” but added the team has managed it and that OEM conversations are increasingly centered on ordering ahead of 2027. Regarding EPA 2027, he said the company’s fleet age, inventory position, and relationships with chassis OEM partners and dealers leave it “very well-positioned,” while the company monitors remaining open questions around warranty and final EPA rulings. Balance sheet, cash flow priorities, and tariff/EPA considerations Eperjesy said the company ended the quarter with net debt of $1.65 billion and last-twelve-month Adjusted EBITDA of more than $408 million, resulting in net leverage “slightly more than 4 times.” He said leverage improved about 30 basis points sequentially and about 80 basis points versus the year-ago quarter. Availability under the asset-based lending facility was $257 million as of March 31, and he said the company could potentially access additional availability by upsizing the facility based on the borrowing base. Free cash flow generation and deleveraging remain key priorities, according to management. Eperjesy said the company expects more than $50 million of levered free cash flow in 2026 and aims to reduce net leverage to “meaningfully below 4 times” by fiscal year-end, while progressing toward a 3x target in 2027. In response to a question from JPMorgan’s Tami Zakaria, Eperjesy said the expected levered free cash flow “would all be used to pay down debt.” On working capital, Eperjesy said inventory was “probably closer to seven and a half months right now” and reiterated the goal of getting below six months on hand. He said the company expects to take “north of $100 million year-over-year out of inventory” as part of its 2026 working capital initiative, while noting most inventory is floor planned and that a $100 million inventory reduction typically equates to roughly $20 million of cash benefit. He said working capital could contribute $30 million to $40 million toward the company’s free cash flow expectation for the year. During Q&A, McMonagle addressed potential impacts from tariffs and the EPA’s 2027 emissions standards. On Section 232 tariffs, he said the company has “inventory on the ground” and is seeing “a little bit of tariff exposure on some of our bodies,” but added the team has managed it and that OEM conversations are increasingly centered on ordering ahead of 2027. Regarding EPA 2027, he said the company’s fleet age, inventory position, and relationships with chassis OEM partners and dealers leave it “very well-positioned,” while the company monitors remaining open questions around warranty and final EPA rulings. Management also fielded questions about potential data center-related pushback affecting T&D demand. McMonagle said the company is still seeing strong demand from customers and that “specific noise around data center doesn’t seem to be impacting our customers and the work that they are planning to start” over coming quarters and years.About Custom Truck One Source NYSE: CTOSCustom Truck One Source, Inc NYSE: CTOS is a North American provider of specialty rental equipment, parts and services. The company's fleet encompasses a wide range of assets, including cranes, aerial work platforms, trench safety and shoring equipment, fluid management solutions, generators and other industrial machinery. Customers rely on Custom Truck One Source to support projects in construction, energy, telecommunications, industrial manufacturing, municipalities and large-scale events. Title: All eyes are on AWS ahead of Amazon earnings as Wall Street looks to see AI spending pay off
URL: https://www.businessinsider.com/amazon-q1-earnings-preview-aws-amzn-stock-ai-capex-2026-4
Time Published: 2026-04-28T18:49:01Z
Full Content:
Amazon crushed expectations in the first quarter, but left investors wanting more on AI. Earnings per share were $2.78, well above the $1.62 estimate, driven in part by its investment in Anthropic. Net sales came in at $181.5 billion, above an estimate of $177.2 billion. The company's closely watched Amazon Web Services segment posted its best growth quarter in about four years. Revenue rose 28% to $37.6 billion. AWS revenue has been a key metric for investors as Amazon has been pouring money into its cloud and AI capabilities in recent quarters. Still, capital expenditures for the quarter totaled $43.2 billion, and the company didn't provide fresh capex guidance. In February, it said it planned to spend $200 billion on capex this year, much of it focused on AI infrastructure. "The headlines look fantastic, but if you peel it back, there are a few negatives that could drag on the stock," David Bartosiak, a stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research, wrote after Amazon reported its results on Wednesday. In a welcome update on Amazon's chip business, CEO Andy Jassy signaled that the company could begin selling full racks of its Trainium AI chips to external providers within the "next couple of years." Jassy is hugely bullish on the chips business, so much so that he took a rare shot at Nvidia when talking about the project in his letter to shareholders earlier this month. The stock has had a strong run in 2026, and investors seem to think the party can continue. Shares were up about 5% in after-hours trading on Wednesday after the earnings report and call ended. Shares of Amazon were up about 5% in after-hours trading as the call concluded. Jassy ended the call by answering a question about how Amazon uses AI internally. He pointed to an example from his letter to shareholders earlier this month, which referenced a major rebuilding project at Amazon Bedrock that would've taken about 40 people about a year to complete. Jassy ended the call by answering a question about how Amazon uses AI internally. He pointed to an example from his letter to shareholders earlier this month, which referenced a major rebuilding project at Amazon Bedrock that would've taken about 40 people about a year to complete. "We took five really smart, AI forward-thinking people building on agentic coding tools," Jassy said. "And those five people rebuilt it in 65 days." Amazon CEO Andy Jassy said the company could begin selling full racks of its Trainium AI chips to external providers within the "next couple of years." That adds a more specific timeline to the comments from his April shareholder letter, where he said such sales could happen "in the future." Currently, Trainium is only available to customers through Amazon's own cloud infrastructure, not via third-party providers. Expanding distribution would put Amazon's chip business in more direct competition with Nvidia's GPUs. Asked about supply chain challenges, Jassy said Amazon has been working with suppliers since the end of last year to shore up its capacity in light of the memory and storage supply chain shortage. "We've done a good job in making sure that we're not capacity constrained there, but we're watching that very closely," he said. Project Leo, Amazon's satellite-based high-speed internet service, could become a "many billion dollar revenue business" over time, Jassy said. Amazon already has 250 satellites in orbit for Project Leo and 20 more launches planned for this year. Like AWS, though, that revenue won't be cheap. Jassy said that the service is "capital intensive up front," though like data centers, Amazon would get "assets that you get to leverage over a long period of time" in return. We're moving into analyst questions. Olsavsky, the CFO, said higher transportation costs related to increased fuel prices are baked into Amazon's Q2 operating income guidance, which was $20 billion to $24 billion, in line with a Wall Street estimate of $22.86 billion. Amazon recently added a fuel and logistics surcharge for some sellers, which Olsavsky said is expected to help offset costs. CFO Brian Olsavsky said that Amazon's capital expenditures totaled $43.2 billion during the first quarter. Jassy said that he wouldn't have an update on Amazon's capital expenditure plans. Amazon said in February that it planned to spend $200 billion on capex this year, much of it focused on AI infrastructure. Jassy said Amazon anticipates good returns on the investments it's making in AI. That's a key topic for investors who are looking for results from the hundreds of billions that Amazon has poured into AWS for AI. Data centers can operate for decades, he said, while other equipment, such as chips, can last as long as six years. Jassy said Amazon anticipates good returns on the investments it's making in AI. That's a key topic for investors who are looking for results from the hundreds of billions that Amazon has poured into AWS for AI. Data centers can operate for decades, he said, while other equipment, such as chips, can last as long as six years. The returns are "cumulatively quite attractive a couple of years after being in service," Jassy said. "However, in times of very high growth, like now, where the capex growth meaningfully outpaces the revenue growth, the early years, free cash flow is challenged." Jassy said monthly active active users for Rufus, its AI shopping assistant, are up 115% and engagement is up 400% year over year. Shoppers use it to research products, track prices, and automatically buy products when they reach a set price, he said. CEO Jassy says Amazon's silicon business would have an annual revenue run rate of $50 billion if it was a standalone company. "As best as we can tell, our custom silicon business is now one of the top three data center chip businesses in the world, and the speed at which we've gotten here is extraordinary," Jassy said. CEO Jassy highlighted Amazon and OpenAI's Tuesday announcement to bring OpenAI's models into Amazon Bedrock. "Open AI has said they're already seeing unprecedented demand for this new product, and we're seeing heavy customer interest as well," Jassy said. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy kicked off the call by pointing to AWS' fast growth. Sales at AWS grew 28% during the quarter, its fastest quarterly growth rate since 2022. "The last time we saw growth at this clip, AWS was roughly half the size," Jassy said. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy kicked off the call by pointing to AWS' fast growth. Sales at AWS grew 28% during the quarter, its fastest quarterly growth rate since 2022. "The last time we saw growth at this clip, AWS was roughly half the size," Jassy said. Three years after AWS launched, it had an annual run rate of $58 million, Jassy said. After three years of the AI wave, he said, AWS' AI run rate is more than $15 billion — about 260 times larger, he said. CEO Andy Jassy, CFO Brian Olsavsky, and head of investor relations Dave Fildes are kicking it off with prepared remarks. "This is a classic Amazon report where the headlines look fantastic, but if you peel it back, there are a few negatives that could drag on the stock," David Bartosiak, a stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research, wrote after Amazon reported its results on Wednesday. Bartosiak pointed to Amazon's $16.8 billion gain on its investment in Anthropic, which boosted net income for the quarter. "Strip that out, and suddenly the quarter looks a lot more 'good' than 'great,'" he wrote. "This is a classic Amazon report where the headlines look fantastic, but if you peel it back, there are a few negatives that could drag on the stock," David Bartosiak, a stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research, wrote after Amazon reported its results on Wednesday. "Strip that out, and suddenly the quarter looks a lot more 'good' than 'great,'" he wrote. While AWS' 28% growth is "impressive," Bartosiak said that some investors might expect even faster growth, given Amazon's commanding position in AI and cloud computing. Amazon's "conservative" operating income guidance and free cash flow decline "dampened sentiment somewhat," EMARKETER principal analyst Sky Canaves wrote after the company reported results on Wednesday. (EMARKETER is a sister company to Business Insider.) A bright spot was Amazon's ecommerce business, which is gaining profitability as it fills orders more efficiently. Amazon's "conservative" operating income guidance and free cash flow decline "dampened sentiment somewhat," EMARKETER principal analyst Sky Canaves wrote after the company reported results on Wednesday. (EMARKETER is a sister company to Business Insider.) A bright spot was Amazon's ecommerce business, which is gaining profitability as it fills orders more efficiently. Amazon's ads business is also expected to "remain a key profit engine for the company," Canaves wrote. The ads business grew 24% year over year in Q1 to $17.2 billion. Sales at AWS rose 28% to $37.6 billion during Amazon's first quarter. That's "our fastest growth in 15 quarters," CEO Andy Jassy wrote in the company's earnings release on Wednesday. Investors had been watching AWS's sales growth, given the hundreds of billions that Amazon has invested in AI. First quarter results: Second quarter guidance: Amazon's Prime Day shopping event is set to take place in June, the company said on Wednesday. In year's past, the sale was typically held in July. Last year's Prime Day happened in the US between July 8 and 11 — a four-day event that was twice as long as in the past. It's not clear how long this year's sales event will be. Free cash flow at Amazon fell 95% in the first quarter from the year-ago period. The company booked $1.2 billion for the trailing 12 months, down from the nearly $26 billion it reported for the first quarter of 2025. Amazon said that the decrease was driven primarily by a year-over-year increase in property and equipment purchases, mostly the result of "investments in artificial intelligence." AWS's first-quarter results were "a masterclass in execution," Investing.com senior analyst Jesse Cohen wrote after the company reported earnings on Wednesday. AWS sales rose 28% during the quarter. "The significant reacceleration in AWS sales growth is the standout story, demonstrating that the cloud optimization cycle is over and enterprises are fully embracing new workloads, especially in AI," Cohen wrote. AWS's first-quarter results were "a masterclass in execution," Investing.com senior analyst Jesse Cohen wrote after the company reported earnings on Wednesday. AWS sales rose 28% during the quarter. "The significant reacceleration in AWS sales growth is the standout story, demonstrating that the cloud optimization cycle is over and enterprises are fully embracing new workloads, especially in AI," Cohen wrote. That's leading to "a stronger-than-expected outlook," Cohen added. Amazon reported earnings per share of $2.78, well above the $1.62 expected by analysts, driven in part by its investment in Anthropic, which contributed $16.8 billion in pre-tax gains during the quarter, the company said Wednesday. Amazon's net income for the quarter was $30.3 billion. Amazon continues to deepen its relationship with Anthropic. In April, the cloud giant said it would invest up to $25 billion more in the startup, adding to the $8 billion it had already committed. Shares of Amazon were off more than 3% in after-hours trading in the minutes after the tech giant reported its first-quarter earnings before erasing those losses. First quarter results: Amazon's custom chip business is "on fire." That's according to Amazon CEO Andy Jassy, who said earlier this month in his letter to shareholders that the company's Trainium chips have surpassed a $20 billion annual run rate. The company has deals with companies like Anthropic to access its Trainium chips via the cloud, but it could go one step further and "sell racks of them to third parties in the future," Jassy said. That would put it in direct competition with Nvidia, whose chips have powered the AI boom. Look out for updates on growing demand for Amazon's custom silicon. Forrester analyst Nikhil Lai said he's looking for signs that Amazon has scaled its ad business beyond search and other sponsored listings on Amazon.com to encompass a broader range of ad budgets, such as those traditionally reserved for TV. Look out for mentions of ad growth on Prime Video. ​​"For the first quarter, the focus isn't just growth rate — it's mix," Lai wrote. "That's especially important as Amazon ramps up AI and infrastructure spending elsewhere in the business." With OpenAI's relationship with Microsoft no longer exclusive, the startup this week said it had agreed to a major expansion of its Amazon partnership. OpenAI's latest models and coding agent are being made available through Amazon Bedrock, and Amazon's cloud boss, Matt Garman, said his company will be a better partner to OpenAI than Microsoft. Shots fired! It's a big win for Amazon, and one we expect it will promote today. Meanwhile, Microsoft investors will want to know just how deep this partnership goes — and whether this spells bad news for Microsoft's Azure business in the long run. Mizuho Americas analyst Lloyd Walmsley says to watch out for weaker operating income due to rising fuel costs, but adds that investors could look past it as a temporary blip. Otherwise, he expects recent upbeat commentary on AI in the firm's quarterly shareholder letter, rising CPU demand, and a surge in valuations on the Magnificent Seven stocks to drive further upside. The bank expects AWS revenue growth in the 29%-31% range, and will stay around 30% year-over-year growth for the rest of 2026. Title: All eyes are on AWS ahead of Amazon earnings as Wall Street looks to see AI spending pay off
URL: https://www.businessinsider.com/amazon-q1-earnings-preview-aws-amzn-stock-ai-capex-2026-4
Time Published: 2026-04-28T18:49:01Z
Full Content:
Amazon crushed expectations in the first quarter, but left investors wanting more on AI. Earnings per share were $2.78, well above the $1.62 estimate, driven in part by its investment in Anthropic. Net sales came in at $181.5 billion, above an estimate of $177.2 billion. The company's closely watched Amazon Web Services segment posted its best growth quarter in about four years. Revenue rose 28% to $37.6 billion. AWS revenue has been a key metric for investors as Amazon has been pouring money into its cloud and AI capabilities in recent quarters. Still, capital expenditures for the quarter totaled $43.2 billion, and the company didn't provide fresh capex guidance. In February, it said it planned to spend $200 billion on capex this year, much of it focused on AI infrastructure. "The headlines look fantastic, but if you peel it back, there are a few negatives that could drag on the stock," David Bartosiak, a stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research, wrote after Amazon reported its results on Wednesday. In a welcome update on Amazon's chip business, CEO Andy Jassy signaled that the company could begin selling full racks of its Trainium AI chips to external providers within the "next couple of years." Jassy is hugely bullish on the chips business, so much so that he took a rare shot at Nvidia when talking about the project in his letter to shareholders earlier this month. The stock has had a strong run in 2026, and investors seem to think the party can continue. Shares were up about 5% in after-hours trading on Wednesday after the earnings report and call ended. Shares of Amazon were up about 5% in after-hours trading as the call concluded. Jassy ended the call by answering a question about how Amazon uses AI internally. He pointed to an example from his letter to shareholders earlier this month, which referenced a major rebuilding project at Amazon Bedrock that would've taken about 40 people about a year to complete. Jassy ended the call by answering a question about how Amazon uses AI internally. He pointed to an example from his letter to shareholders earlier this month, which referenced a major rebuilding project at Amazon Bedrock that would've taken about 40 people about a year to complete. "We took five really smart, AI forward-thinking people building on agentic coding tools," Jassy said. "And those five people rebuilt it in 65 days." Amazon CEO Andy Jassy said the company could begin selling full racks of its Trainium AI chips to external providers within the "next couple of years." That adds a more specific timeline to the comments from his April shareholder letter, where he said such sales could happen "in the future." Currently, Trainium is only available to customers through Amazon's own cloud infrastructure, not via third-party providers. Expanding distribution would put Amazon's chip business in more direct competition with Nvidia's GPUs. Asked about supply chain challenges, Jassy said Amazon has been working with suppliers since the end of last year to shore up its capacity in light of the memory and storage supply chain shortage. "We've done a good job in making sure that we're not capacity constrained there, but we're watching that very closely," he said. Project Leo, Amazon's satellite-based high-speed internet service, could become a "many billion dollar revenue business" over time, Jassy said. Amazon already has 250 satellites in orbit for Project Leo and 20 more launches planned for this year. Like AWS, though, that revenue won't be cheap. Jassy said that the service is "capital intensive up front," though like data centers, Amazon would get "assets that you get to leverage over a long period of time" in return. We're moving into analyst questions. Olsavsky, the CFO, said higher transportation costs related to increased fuel prices are baked into Amazon's Q2 operating income guidance, which was $20 billion to $24 billion, in line with a Wall Street estimate of $22.86 billion. Amazon recently added a fuel and logistics surcharge for some sellers, which Olsavsky said is expected to help offset costs. CFO Brian Olsavsky said that Amazon's capital expenditures totaled $43.2 billion during the first quarter. Jassy said that he wouldn't have an update on Amazon's capital expenditure plans. Amazon said in February that it planned to spend $200 billion on capex this year, much of it focused on AI infrastructure. Jassy said Amazon anticipates good returns on the investments it's making in AI. That's a key topic for investors who are looking for results from the hundreds of billions that Amazon has poured into AWS for AI. Data centers can operate for decades, he said, while other equipment, such as chips, can last as long as six years. Jassy said Amazon anticipates good returns on the investments it's making in AI. That's a key topic for investors who are looking for results from the hundreds of billions that Amazon has poured into AWS for AI. Data centers can operate for decades, he said, while other equipment, such as chips, can last as long as six years. The returns are "cumulatively quite attractive a couple of years after being in service," Jassy said. "However, in times of very high growth, like now, where the capex growth meaningfully outpaces the revenue growth, the early years, free cash flow is challenged." Jassy said monthly active active users for Rufus, its AI shopping assistant, are up 115% and engagement is up 400% year over year. Shoppers use it to research products, track prices, and automatically buy products when they reach a set price, he said. CEO Jassy says Amazon's silicon business would have an annual revenue run rate of $50 billion if it was a standalone company. "As best as we can tell, our custom silicon business is now one of the top three data center chip businesses in the world, and the speed at which we've gotten here is extraordinary," Jassy said. CEO Jassy highlighted Amazon and OpenAI's Tuesday announcement to bring OpenAI's models into Amazon Bedrock. "Open AI has said they're already seeing unprecedented demand for this new product, and we're seeing heavy customer interest as well," Jassy said. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy kicked off the call by pointing to AWS' fast growth. Sales at AWS grew 28% during the quarter, its fastest quarterly growth rate since 2022. "The last time we saw growth at this clip, AWS was roughly half the size," Jassy said. Amazon CEO Andy Jassy kicked off the call by pointing to AWS' fast growth. Sales at AWS grew 28% during the quarter, its fastest quarterly growth rate since 2022. "The last time we saw growth at this clip, AWS was roughly half the size," Jassy said. Three years after AWS launched, it had an annual run rate of $58 million, Jassy said. After three years of the AI wave, he said, AWS' AI run rate is more than $15 billion — about 260 times larger, he said. CEO Andy Jassy, CFO Brian Olsavsky, and head of investor relations Dave Fildes are kicking it off with prepared remarks. "This is a classic Amazon report where the headlines look fantastic, but if you peel it back, there are a few negatives that could drag on the stock," David Bartosiak, a stock strategist at Zacks Investment Research, wrote after Amazon reported its results on Wednesday. Bartosiak pointed to Amazon's $16.8 billion gain on its investment in Anthropic, which boosted net income for the quarter. "Strip that out, and suddenly the quarter looks a lot more 'good' than 'great,'" he wrote. Sales at AWS rose 28% to $37.6 billion during Amazon's first quarter. That's "our fastest growth in 15 quarters," CEO Andy Jassy wrote in the company's earnings release on Wednesday. Investors had been watching AWS's sales growth, given the hundreds of billions that Amazon has invested in AI. First quarter results: Second quarter guidance: Amazon's Prime Day shopping event is set to take place in June, the company said on Wednesday. In year's past, the sale was typically held in July. Last year's Prime Day happened in the US between July 8 and 11 — a four-day event that was twice as long as in the past. It's not clear how long this year's sales event will be. Free cash flow at Amazon fell 95% in the first quarter from the year-ago period. The company booked $1.2 billion for the trailing 12 months, down from the nearly $26 billion it reported for the first quarter of 2025. Amazon said that the decrease was driven primarily by a year-over-year increase in property and equipment purchases, mostly the result of "investments in artificial intelligence." "The significant reacceleration in AWS sales growth is the standout story, demonstrating that the cloud optimization cycle is over and enterprises are fully embracing new workloads, especially in AI," Cohen wrote. Amazon reported earnings per share of $2.78, well above the $1.62 expected by analysts, driven in part by its investment in Anthropic, which contributed $16.8 billion in pre-tax gains during the quarter, the company said Wednesday. Amazon's net income for the quarter was $30.3 billion. Amazon continues to deepen its relationship with Anthropic. In April, the cloud giant said it would invest up to $25 billion more in the startup, adding to the $8 billion it had already committed. Shares of Amazon were off more than 3% in after-hours trading in the minutes after the tech giant reported its first-quarter earnings before erasing those losses. First quarter results: Amazon's custom chip business is "on fire." That's according to Amazon CEO Andy Jassy, who said earlier this month in his letter to shareholders that the company's Trainium chips have surpassed a $20 billion annual run rate. The company has deals with companies like Anthropic to access its Trainium chips via the cloud, but it could go one step further and "sell racks of them to third parties in the future," Jassy said. That would put it in direct competition with Nvidia, whose chips have powered the AI boom. Look out for updates on growing demand for Amazon's custom silicon. Forrester analyst Nikhil Lai said he's looking for signs that Amazon has scaled its ad business beyond search and other sponsored listings on Amazon.com to encompass a broader range of ad budgets, such as those traditionally reserved for TV. Look out for mentions of ad growth on Prime Video. ​​"For the first quarter, the focus isn't just growth rate — it's mix," Lai wrote. "That's especially important as Amazon ramps up AI and infrastructure spending elsewhere in the business." With OpenAI's relationship with Microsoft no longer exclusive, the startup this week said it had agreed to a major expansion of its Amazon partnership. OpenAI's latest models and coding agent are being made available through Amazon Bedrock, and Amazon's cloud boss, Matt Garman, said his company will be a better partner to OpenAI than Microsoft. Shots fired! It's a big win for Amazon, and one we expect it will promote today. Meanwhile, Microsoft investors will want to know just how deep this partnership goes — and whether this spells bad news for Microsoft's Azure business in the long run. Mizuho Americas analyst Lloyd Walmsley says to watch out for weaker operating income due to rising fuel costs, but adds that investors could look past it as a temporary blip. Otherwise, he expects recent upbeat commentary on AI in the firm's quarterly shareholder letter, rising CPU demand, and a surge in valuations on the Magnificent Seven stocks to drive further upside.
